When Claude makes an invalid tool call (malformed inputs, wrong parameter types), it will automatically retry 2-3 times with corrections before apologizing to the user. This built-in retry behavior means:

  • You do not need to implement client-side retry logic for input validation errors
  • Claude learns from the error message to adjust its next attempt
  • After exhausting retries, Claude generates a natural language apology explaining the failure
Design implication: The retry behavior makes instructive error messages even more important. Each retry uses your error message as context for the corrected attempt.

Server Tool Error Codes

Server-executed tools (web_search, code_execution, etc.) have their own error taxonomy. These errors are handled transparently by Claude -- you never construct a tool_result for server tools.

Error CodeMeaning
too_many_requestsRate limit exceeded
invalid_inputInvalid search query parameter
max_uses_exceededMaximum tool uses exceeded for this conversation
query_too_longQuery exceeds maximum length
unavailableInternal error occurred

Access Failures vs Valid Empty Results

It is critical to distinguish between these two cases:

Scenariois_errorExample
Tool succeeded but found nothingfalseSearch returned 0 results (valid empty response)
Tool failed to executetrueDatabase connection timeout, API rate limit, permission denied

Returning is_error: true for a valid empty result causes Claude to retry unnecessarily. Conversely, returning is_error: false for a real failure prevents Claude from attempting recovery.

// Valid empty result  --  NOT an error
{
  "type": "tool_result",
  "tool_use_id": "toolu_abc123",
  "content": "No matching records found for query 'xyz'."
}

// Access failure  --  IS an error
{
  "type": "tool_result",
  "tool_use_id": "toolu_abc123",
  "content": "DatabaseError: Connection timed out after 30s. The database server at db.example.com:5432 is not responding.",
  "is_error": true
}

Local Recovery Before Escalation

In agentic systems, tools should attempt local recovery before reporting errors back to the model. Consider implementing:

  • Connection retries with exponential backoff within the tool handler
  • Fallback data sources when the primary source is unavailable
  • Graceful degradation returning partial results with a warning rather than failing entirely

Only escalate to Claude (via is_error: true) when local recovery options are exhausted. This reduces unnecessary retry loops in the agentic conversation.

MCP Error Mechanisms

In the MCP protocol, there are two distinct error mechanisms to be aware of:

TypeMechanismWhen Used
Protocol ErrorsJSON-RPC error response with code/messageUnknown tools (-32602), invalid arguments (-32602), server errors (-32603), resource not found (-32002)
Execution ErrorsisError: true in tool resultAPI failures, invalid input data, business logic errors
// Protocol error (JSON-RPC)
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 3, "error": {"code": -32602, "message": "Unknown tool: invalid_tool_name"}}

// Execution error (tool result)
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 4, "result": {"content": [{"type": "text", "text": "API rate limit exceeded"}], "isError": true}}
doc_label: How Tool Use Works - Error Handling
summary: Covers the is_error:true pattern for tool result error reporting, Claude's automatic 2-3 retry self-correction behavior, server tool error codes, and the critical distinction between access failures and valid empty results. Also covers MCP's two error mechanisms: protocol-level JSON-RPC errors and execution-level isError.

key_concepts.definition: Boolean field on tool_result blocks indicating execution failure. Claude incorporates the error content into its reasoning and may retry with corrections.
Claude automatically retries invalid tool calls 2-3 times with corrections before apologizing. Error messages serve as context for each retry attempt.
Access failures (connection timeout, permission denied) should use is_error:true. Valid empty results (0 search matches) should NOT use is_error:true.
Standard JSON-RPC error codes: -32602 for unknown tools/invalid arguments, -32603 for server errors, -32002 for resource not found.
Tools should attempt connection retries, fallback sources, and graceful degradation before escalating errors to the model via is_error:true.

title: MCP Architecture and Primitives
content:
Three MCP Primitives

MCP defines three core primitives, each with a distinct control model that determines who decides when they are used:

PrimitiveControl ModelDescriptionAnalogy
ToolsModel-controlledLLM discovers and invokes automatically based on contextFunctions the AI can call
ResourcesApplication-drivenHost application determines how to incorporate contextGET endpoints for data
PromptsUser-controlledUser explicitly selects (e.g., slash commands in Claude Code)Reusable templates
Exam-critical mnemonic: Tools = Model, Resources = Application, Prompts = User. The control model determines who initiates the action.

Tools (Model-Controlled)

MCP Tools are discovered via tools/list and invoked via tools/call. Each tool definition includes name, optional title, description, inputSchema, optional outputSchema, and annotations.

Resources (Application-Driven)

Resources are identified by URIs following RFC 3986. Common schemes include https://, file://, git://, and custom schemes. Resource templates use RFC 6570 URI templates for parameterized access:

{
  "uriTemplate": "file:///{path}",
  "name": "Project Files",
  "description": "Access files in the project directory",
  "mimeType": "application/octet-stream"
}

Resources support subscriptions (resources/subscribe) and change notifications.

Prompts (User-Controlled)

Prompts are discoverable templates with optional arguments. In Claude Code, they appear as /mcp__servername__promptname slash commands.

Tool Annotations

Tool annotations provide metadata about tool behavior. Clients MUST consider annotations untrusted unless from trusted servers.

PropertyTypeDefaultMeaning
readOnlyHintbooleanfalseTool does not modify its environment
destructiveHintbooleantrueTool may perform destructive updates
idempotentHintbooleanfalseRepeated calls with same args have no additional effect
openWorldHintbooleantrueTool interacts with external entities

In the Agent SDK, readOnlyHint: true enables parallel execution of the tool alongside other read-only tools.

Two Error Mechanisms

MCP has two distinct error pathways:

  1. Protocol Errors: Standard JSON-RPC error responses for structural issues
    • Unknown tool: -32602
    • Invalid arguments: -32602
    • Server error: -32603
    • Resource not found: -32002
  2. Execution Errors: isError: true in tool results for runtime failures (API failures, business logic errors)
// Protocol error
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 3,
 "error": {"code": -32602, "message": "Unknown tool: invalid_name"}}

// Execution error
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 4,
 "result": {"content": [{"type": "text", "text": "Rate limit exceeded"}], "isError": true}}

Content Annotations

All MCP content types support annotations that guide how content is used:

  • audience: array of "user" and/or "assistant" -- controls who sees the content
  • priority: number 0.0 to 1.0 (1 = most important) -- suggests importance for context management
  • lastModified: ISO 8601 timestamp

MCP Sampling

Sampling allows MCP servers to request LLM completions from clients, enabling nested agentic behaviors. The server sends a sampling/createMessage request with messages, model preferences, and parameters. The client maintains control over model access and selection.

Security requirement: There SHOULD always be a human in the loop for sampling requests. Applications SHOULD provide UI to review requests, allow editing of prompts, and present responses for review before returning to the server.

Model preferences use hints (advisory model suggestions), costPriority, speedPriority, and intelligencePriority (all 0-1 scales).

MCP Roots

Roots define filesystem boundaries where servers can operate. Clients expose roots (always file:// URIs) to servers, which should respect these boundaries. Roots support change notifications via notifications/roots/list_changed.
summary: Covers the three MCP primitives (Tools=Model-controlled, Resources=Application-driven, Prompts=User-controlled), tool annotations (readOnlyHint, destructiveHint, idempotentHint, openWorldHint), resource templates using RFC 6570, the two error mechanisms (protocol JSON-RPC vs execution isError), content annotations, MCP sampling for nested LLM calls, and MCP roots for filesystem boundaries.

key_concepts.definition: Tools (model-controlled, LLM invokes), Resources (application-driven, host decides), Prompts (user-controlled, explicit selection like slash commands).
Metadata hints: readOnlyHint (default false), destructiveHint (default true), idempotentHint (default false), openWorldHint (default true). Clients must treat as untrusted.
Parameterized resource URIs using RFC 6570 URI Templates, allowing dynamic resource access like file:///{path}.
Mechanism allowing MCP servers to request LLM completions from clients via sampling/createMessage. Requires human-in-the-loop for security.
Metadata on content types: audience (user/assistant array), priority (0.0-1.0), lastModified (ISO 8601). Guide content routing and importance.

title: MCP Transports and Communication
content:
JSON-RPC 2.0 over UTF-8

All MCP messages use JSON-RPC 2.0 encoding and MUST be UTF-8 encoded. There are three message types:

TypeHas idPurpose
RequestYes (string or integer, MUST NOT be null)Expects a response
ResponseYes (matches request)Reply with result OR error
NotificationNoOne-way, no reply expected
// Request
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "method": "tools/list", "params": {}}

// Response
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "result": {"tools": [...]}}

// Notification (no id)
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "method": "notifications/progress", "params": {"progressToken": "abc", "progress": 50}}

stdio Transport

The client launches the MCP server as a subprocess. Communication flows through standard I/O:

  • Server reads JSON-RPC messages from stdin
  • Server writes responses to stdout
  • Messages are delimited by newlines and MUST NOT contain embedded newlines
  • Server MAY write to stderr for logging (clients may capture, forward, or ignore)
  • Server MUST NOT write non-MCP content to stdout

stdio is the simplest transport, best for local servers and single-client scenarios. Clients SHOULD support stdio whenever possible.

# Adding a stdio server in Claude Code
claude mcp add my-server -- node /path/to/server.js --arg1 value1

Streamable HTTP Transport

Naming precision: The correct name is Streamable HTTP, not just "HTTP". It replaces the deprecated HTTP+SSE transport from protocol version 2024-11-05.

Streamable HTTP operates differently from stdio:

  • Server runs as an independent process handling multiple client connections
  • Provides a single HTTP endpoint (the "MCP endpoint") supporting both POST and GET
  • Client sends JSON-RPC messages via HTTP POST
  • Client MUST include Accept header listing both application/json and text/event-stream
  • Client MUST include MCP-Protocol-Version: 2025-06-18 header

POST Response Modes

  • For notifications/responses: server returns 202 Accepted (no body)
  • For requests: server returns either application/json (single response) or text/event-stream (SSE stream)
  • SSE streams may include server requests/notifications before the final response

Session Management (Mcp-Session-Id)

Streamable HTTP supports stateful sessions:

  • Server MAY assign session ID via Mcp-Session-Id header on the InitializeResult response
  • Session ID MUST only contain visible ASCII characters (0x21 to 0x7E)
  • Session ID SHOULD be globally unique and cryptographically secure (UUID, JWT, hash)
  • Client MUST include Mcp-Session-Id on all subsequent HTTP requests
  • Server responds with HTTP 404 to expired/terminated session IDs
  • Client SHOULD send HTTP DELETE to explicitly terminate sessions
  • On 404, client MUST start a new session with fresh InitializeRequest

Security

Critical security requirements for Streamable HTTP servers:

  • Servers MUST validate Origin header on all connections (prevents DNS rebinding attacks)
  • Local servers SHOULD bind only to localhost (127.0.0.1)
  • Servers SHOULD implement proper authentication

Resumability

Streamable HTTP supports message resumption for reliability:

  • Servers MAY attach id fields to SSE events (must be globally unique within session)
  • Clients resume interrupted streams by sending GET with Last-Event-ID header
  • Server MAY replay missed messages from the stream
  • Event IDs serve as cursors within each stream

Reconnection in Claude Code

Claude Code implements automatic reconnection for remote MCP servers:

PropertyValue
StrategyExponential backoff
Starting delay1 second
Progression1s → 2s → 4s → 8s → 16s
Maximum attempts5
After 5 failuresServer marked as failed; manual retry from /mcp
stdio serversNOT auto-reconnected
# Transport options in Claude Code
claude mcp add --transport http stripe https://mcp.stripe.com     # Streamable HTTP
claude mcp add --transport sse legacy https://old.example.com/sse  # SSE (deprecated)
claude mcp add my-local -- python server.py                        # stdio
summary: Covers MCP transport layers: JSON-RPC 2.0 over UTF-8 encoding, stdio transport for local subprocess communication, and Streamable HTTP (replacing deprecated SSE) for remote servers. Details session management via Mcp-Session-Id, security requirements (Origin validation, localhost binding), resumability via Last-Event-ID, and Claude Code's exponential backoff reconnection (5 attempts).

key_concepts.definition: MCP's message encoding standard. All messages are UTF-8 encoded. Three types: Requests (have id), Responses (match request id), Notifications (no id).
Client launches server as subprocess. Communication via stdin/stdout, newline-delimited. stderr for logging. Best for local, single-client scenarios.
Replaces deprecated HTTP+SSE. Single endpoint supports POST (client→server) and GET (server-initiated SSE). Requires Accept header with both application/json and text/event-stream.
Session management header. Server assigns on InitializeResult. Client includes on all subsequent requests. 404 response means session expired  --  must reinitialize.
Claude Code reconnects HTTP/SSE servers with 1s→2s→4s→8s→16s delays, up to 5 attempts. stdio servers are NOT auto-reconnected.

title: MCP Server Configuration in Claude Code
content:
Three Scopes

MCP servers in Claude Code can be configured at three scopes, each with different visibility and storage:

ScopeLoads InShared with TeamStored In
Local (default)Current project onlyNo~/.claude.json (under project path)
ProjectCurrent project onlyYes, via version control.mcp.json in project root
UserAll your projectsNo~/.claude.json (global section)
Naming gotcha: MCP "local" scope stores in ~/.claude.json (home directory, per-project section). This differs from general local settings which use .claude/settings.local.json (project directory).

Scope Precedence

When the same server name exists at multiple scopes, the highest-priority scope wins:

  1. Local scope (highest)
  2. Project scope
  3. User scope
  4. Plugin-provided servers
  5. claude.ai connectors (lowest)

CLI Commands

# Add servers at different scopes
claude mcp add --transport http stripe https://mcp.stripe.com           # Local (default)
claude mcp add --transport http stripe --scope local https://mcp.stripe.com  # Explicit local
claude mcp add --transport http paypal --scope project https://mcp.paypal.com/mcp  # Project
claude mcp add --transport http hubspot --scope user https://mcp.hubspot.com  # User

# stdio transport
claude mcp add my-db -- python /path/to/db-server.py --config db.json
claude mcp add --transport stdio --env DB_URL=postgres://... my-db -- python server.py

# Management
claude mcp list                      # Show all configured servers
claude mcp get stripe                # Show details for specific server
claude mcp remove stripe             # Remove a server
claude mcp reset-project-choices     # Reset project-scope approval choices
Option ordering: All options must come BEFORE the server name. -- separates the name from the command/args for stdio servers.

Environment Variable Expansion

The .mcp.json file supports environment variable expansion in command, args, env, url, and headers fields:

SyntaxBehavior
${VAR}Expands to value of environment variable VAR
${VAR:-default}Expands to VAR if set, otherwise uses default value
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "api-server": {
      "type": "http",
      "url": "${API_BASE_URL:-https://api.example.com}/mcp",
      "headers": {
        "Authorization": "Bearer ${API_KEY}"
      }
    },
    "database-tools": {
      "command": "python",
      "args": ["${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/servers/db-server.py"],
      "env": {
        "DB_URL": "${DB_URL}"
      }
    }
  }
}
Failure mode: If a required environment variable is not set and has no default value, Claude Code will fail to parse the config. Always provide defaults for optional variables.

Plugin-specific variables: ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} (bundled plugin files) and ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA} (persistent data directory).

Managed MCP (managed-mcp.json)

Enterprise administrators can deploy managed MCP configurations that users cannot modify:

  • macOS: /Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/managed-mcp.json
  • Linux/WSL: /etc/claude-code/managed-mcp.json
  • Windows: C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\managed-mcp.json

Users cannot add, modify, or use any servers other than those defined in managed-mcp.json. Alternatively, policy-based allowlists/denylists use allowedMcpServers and deniedMcpServers with restrictions matching by serverName, serverCommand, or serverUrl. Denylist takes absolute precedence over allowlists.

Tool Search

By default, MCP tool definitions are deferred -- only tool names load at session start. Full schemas are loaded on demand via the ToolSearch tool. This is controlled by the ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH environment variable:

ValueBehavior
(unset) or trueAll MCP tools deferred, loaded on demand (default)
autoDeferred if tools exceed 10% of context window
auto:NCustom percentage threshold (0-100)
falseAll MCP tools loaded upfront

Tool search requires Sonnet 4+ or Opus 4+. Haiku models do NOT support tool search.

MCP Output Limits

ThresholdValue
Warning10,000 tokens
Default maximum25,000 tokens
Configurable viaMAX_MCP_OUTPUT_TOKENS env var
Per-tool override_meta["anthropic/maxResultSizeChars"] in tool's tools/list entry
Maximum per-tool500,000 characters

The MCP_TIMEOUT environment variable controls the startup timeout for MCP server connections.
summary: Covers MCP server configuration in Claude Code across three scopes (local, project, user) with precedence rules. Details CLI commands for server management, environment variable expansion syntax (${VAR} and ${VAR:-default}), managed-mcp.json for enterprise control, tool search for deferred loading, and MCP output limits (10K warning, 25K default, 500K max per-tool).

key_concepts.definition: Local (default, ~/.claude.json per-project), Project (.mcp.json, version-controlled), User (~/.claude.json global). Precedence: local > project > user > plugin > claude.ai.
${VAR} expands to variable value, ${VAR:-default} provides fallback. Supported in command, args, env, url, headers fields. Missing required vars fail config parsing.
Enterprise-deployed MCP configuration at system paths. Users cannot add/modify servers beyond what is defined. Denylist takes absolute precedence over allowlist.
Controls MCP tool schema loading. Default: deferred on demand. auto: threshold-based. false: all upfront. Requires Sonnet 4+/Opus 4+ (not Haiku).
Warning at 10K tokens, default max 25K tokens (MAX_MCP_OUTPUT_TOKENS). Per-tool override via _meta["anthropic/maxResultSizeChars"] up to 500K chars.

title: Claude Code Built-in Tools
content:
Core Tools

Claude Code ships with six core tools that handle all fundamental file and search operations:

ToolPurposeWhen to UsePermission
ReadRead file contents from filesystemWhen you know the specific file path; supports images, PDFs (with pages param), notebooks; up to 2000 lines by defaultNo
WriteCreate new files or complete rewritesNew files only; must Read first for existing files; overwrites entirelyYes
EditExact string replacements in filesModifying existing files (preferred over Write); old_string must be unique in file; supports replace_allYes
BashExecute shell commandsRunning git, npm, docker, build commands; working directory persists between callsYes
GrepSearch file contents (built on ripgrep)Finding patterns in code; regex, file type filtering, context lines; modes: content, files_with_matches, countNo
GlobFast file pattern matchingFinding files by name pattern (e.g., **/*.ts); sorted by modification timeNo

Selection Criteria: Why Each Over Shell

Claude Code enforces specific tool selection over shell equivalents. This is a design decision for reliability and context efficiency:

TaskUseNOT
File search by nameGlobfind or ls via Bash
Content searchGrepgrep or rg via Bash
Read filesReadcat/head/tail via Bash
Edit filesEditsed/awk via Bash
Write new filesWriteecho via Bash
Shell operationsBash(no alternative)
Design rationale: Built-in tools provide structured input/output, better error handling, and optimized context usage compared to raw shell commands. The Grep tool returns structured results with line numbers and context, while grep via Bash produces unstructured text that consumes more context.

Additional Tools

ToolPurposePermission
ToolSearchDeferred tool discovery for MCP tools; searches by name/keywords to load schemas on demandNo
WebFetchFetch and process web page contentYes
WebSearchWeb search capabilityYes
AgentSpawn subagents with own context window, custom tools, independent permissionsNo
SkillExecute skills (reusable prompts/workflows) in main conversationYes
NotebookEditModify Jupyter notebook cellsYes
MonitorWatch background processes (v2.1.98+); each output line delivered as notificationYes
PowerShellNative PowerShell execution (requires CLAUDE_CODE_USE_POWERSHELL_TOOL=1)Yes

Agent SDK: tools vs allowedTools vs disallowedTools

The Agent SDK provides three distinct mechanisms for controlling tool access, operating at different layers:

OptionLayerEffect
tools: ["Read", "Grep"]AvailabilityOnly listed built-ins in context. Unlisted tools completely removed. MCP tools unaffected
tools: []AvailabilityAll built-ins removed. Only MCP tools remain
allowedToolsPermissionListed tools run without permission prompt. Unlisted tools still available but need permission
disallowedToolsPermissionEvery call denied. Tool stays in context (Claude may waste a turn attempting to use it)
Best practice: Prefer tools over disallowedTools to remove tools from context entirely. With disallowedTools, the tool definition still consumes context tokens and Claude may still attempt to call it, wasting a turn.
# Read-only analysis agent
options = ClaudeAgentOptions(
    tools=["Read", "Glob", "Grep"],
    allowed_tools=["Read", "Glob", "Grep"]
)

# Full automation with MCP
options = ClaudeAgentOptions(
    tools=["Read", "Edit", "Bash", "Glob", "Grep"],
    allowed_tools=["mcp__github__*", "Read", "Edit", "Bash"],
    mcp_servers={"github": {"type": "http", "url": "https://mcp.github.com"}}
)

Tool Annotations for Custom Tools

When creating custom tools in the Agent SDK, annotations provide metadata that affects execution behavior:

AnnotationDefaultEffect
readOnlyHint: truefalseEnables parallel execution alongside other read-only tools
destructiveHint: truetrueInformational -- may influence permission decisions
idempotentHint: truefalseInformational -- repeated calls have no additional effect
openWorldHint: truetrueInformational -- interacts with external entities
from claude_agent_sdk import tool

@tool(
    "check_status",
    "Check the deployment status of a service",
    {"service": str},
    annotations={"readOnlyHint": True, "openWorldHint": True}
)
async def check_status(args: dict) -> dict:
    return {"content": [{"type": "text", "text": f"Service {args['service']} is healthy"}]}
Parallel execution: Marking custom tools with readOnlyHint: true is the key to enabling parallel execution in the Agent SDK. Without it, custom tools default to sequential execution.
summary: Covers Claude Code's six core built-in tools (Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob) with their selection criteria over shell equivalents. Details additional tools (ToolSearch, WebFetch, Agent, Skill, Monitor, PowerShell), the Agent SDK's three-layer tool access control (tools, allowedTools, disallowedTools), and tool annotations that control parallel execution behavior.

key_concepts.definition: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob  --  the six fundamental tools for file operations, shell execution, and search in Claude Code.
Claude Code enforces using built-in tools (Glob, Grep, Read, Edit, Write) instead of shell equivalents (find, grep, cat, sed, echo) for reliability and context efficiency.
tools controls availability (removes from context), allowedTools controls permissions (auto-approve), disallowedTools denies but keeps in context. Prefer tools over disallowedTools.
Setting readOnlyHint:true on custom tools enables parallel execution alongside other read-only tools. Default is false (sequential execution).
Deferred tool discovery that loads MCP tool schemas on demand, keeping initial context usage low. Only tool names load at session start.

title: CLAUDE.md Hierarchy and Memory System
content:
CLAUDE.md: The Persistent Context Layer

CLAUDE.md files are the primary mechanism for providing persistent, project-level instructions to Claude Code. Unlike conversation history which is ephemeral, CLAUDE.md content is loaded into every session and survives compaction. Understanding the four-scope hierarchy, import system, and auto-memory behavior is essential for effective Claude Code configuration.

The Four Scopes (Resolution Order)

Claude Code loads CLAUDE.md files from four distinct scopes. All discovered files are concatenated, not overriding each other — every scope's instructions are additive.

ScopeLocationPurposeShared With
Managed PolicymacOS: /Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/CLAUDE.md
Linux/WSL: /etc/claude-code/CLAUDE.md
Windows: C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\CLAUDE.md		Organization-wide instructions managed by IT/DevOpsAll users in org
Project./CLAUDE.md or ./.claude/CLAUDE.mdTeam-shared instructionsTeam via source control
User~/.claude/CLAUDE.mdPersonal preferences for all projectsJust you (all projects)
Local./CLAUDE.local.mdPersonal project-specific preferencesJust you (current project; add to .gitignore)
Exam-Critical: Managed Policy Cannot Be Excluded

The managed policy CLAUDE.md cannot be excluded by claudeMdExcludes or any user/project setting. This is a deliberate security constraint for enterprise environments.

Directory Walk-Up Behavior

Claude Code walks up the directory tree from the current working directory. Running in foo/bar/ loads foo/bar/CLAUDE.md, then foo/CLAUDE.md, then the root. Within each directory, CLAUDE.local.md is appended after CLAUDE.md. Subdirectory CLAUDE.md files load on demand when Claude reads files in those subdirectories.

@import Syntax

Import additional files into CLAUDE.md context using the @path/to/file syntax. This is not @import — just the @ prefix followed by the path.

# Project Instructions
See @README.md for project overview.
Git workflow: @docs/git-instructions.md
Personal prefs: @~/.claude/my-project-instructions.md
FeatureDetail
Path typesBoth relative and absolute paths supported
Relative resolutionRelative to the file containing the import, NOT the working directory
Recursive depthImported files can import others, max 5 hops
ApprovalFirst encounter of external imports shows approval dialog; declining permanently disables those imports

claudeMdExcludes Setting

Skip specific CLAUDE.md files by path or glob pattern. Patterns are matched against absolute file paths. Arrays merge across settings layers.

{
  "claudeMdExcludes": [
    "**/monorepo/CLAUDE.md",
    "/home/user/monorepo/other-team/.claude/rules/**"
  ]
}

AGENTS.md Compatibility

Claude Code reads CLAUDE.md, not AGENTS.md. To share instructions across tools that use the open-standard AGENTS.md, import it:

# CLAUDE.md
@AGENTS.md

## Claude Code-Specific
Use plan mode for changes under src/billing/.

Auto Memory (MEMORY.md)

Claude automatically saves learnings (build commands, debugging insights, architecture notes) to a separate memory system at ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/.

AspectCLAUDE.mdAuto Memory
Who writes itYou (the developer)Claude
Loaded at startupFull contentFirst 200 lines or 25KB (whichever first)
Topic filesN/ALoaded on demand (not at startup)
ToggleAlways on/memory command or autoMemoryEnabled: false
Env disableN/ACLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_AUTO_MEMORY=1

Compaction Behavior

Project-root CLAUDE.md survives compaction — it is re-read from disk and re-injected. Nested subdirectory CLAUDE.md files are not automatically re-injected; they reload when Claude reads files in that subdirectory again. Use a "Compact Instructions" section in CLAUDE.md for content that must survive summarization.

The /init Command

/init generates a starting CLAUDE.md automatically. If one exists, it suggests improvements without overwriting. Enable the interactive multi-phase flow (CLAUDE.md, skills, hooks) with CLAUDE_CODE_NEW_INIT=1.

Writing Effective Instructions

  • Target under 200 lines per CLAUDE.md file
  • Use markdown headers and bullets for scannability
  • Be specific and verifiable — avoid vague guidance
  • Include: build commands Claude can't guess, non-default code style, test instructions, architecture decisions
  • Exclude: anything Claude can infer from code, standard conventions, frequently changing info
  • Use emphasis (IMPORTANT, YOU MUST) for critical rules
Anti-Pattern: Over-Specified CLAUDE.md

If CLAUDE.md exceeds ~200 lines, rules get lost in context. Move procedures and reference material into skills (loaded on-demand) and keep CLAUDE.md for facts and conventions only.

Common Anti-Patterns
  • Anti-pattern: Monolithic CLAUDE.md that tries to contain all instructions in a single file
    Instead: Use modular architecture with @path/to/file imports and .claude/rules/ for path-scoped rules; keep the root CLAUDE.md under 200 lines
  • Anti-pattern: Using plan mode for single-file fixes with a clear stack trace
    Instead: Reserve plan mode for cross-cutting changes that span multiple files; for targeted fixes with clear error messages, let Claude work directly
  • Anti-pattern: Running Claude Code in CI without the -p flag, causing it to hang waiting for interactive input
    Instead: Always use claude -p "prompt" (print mode) in CI/CD pipelines -- it runs non-interactively and exits with the result
  • Anti-pattern: Resuming sessions with stale results without verification
    Instead: After resuming or compacting, re-run verification tests before continuing work; stale context from previous sessions may no longer reflect the current codebase state
summary: CLAUDE.md files provide persistent project instructions loaded every session across four scopes: managed policy (cannot be excluded), project, user, and local (CLAUDE.local.md, gitignored). Files walk up the directory tree and are concatenated. The @path/to/file import syntax supports relative paths resolved from the containing file, with a maximum depth of 5 hops. Auto memory (MEMORY.md) loads the first 200 lines or 25KB at session start. Project-root CLAUDE.md survives compaction; subdirectory files do not.

key_concepts.definition: Managed policy (org-wide, cannot be excluded), project (./CLAUDE.md), user (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md), and local (./CLAUDE.local.md, gitignored). All are concatenated additively.
Use @path/to/file (not @import) to include additional files. Relative paths resolve from the containing file. Max 5 recursive hops.
Personal project-specific instructions that should be added to .gitignore. Appended after CLAUDE.md within the same directory.
Settings array that skips specific CLAUDE.md files by glob pattern. Managed policy CLAUDE.md cannot be excluded.
Claude's self-managed memory at ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/. First 200 lines or 25KB loaded at startup. Topic files load on demand.
Project-root CLAUDE.md is re-read from disk and re-injected after compaction. Subdirectory CLAUDE.md files are NOT re-injected automatically.

title: Rules Directory and Path-Scoped Instructions
content:
Organizing Instructions with .claude/rules/

The .claude/rules/ directory provides a structured way to organize project instructions into modular, topic-specific files. Unlike a monolithic CLAUDE.md, rules can be path-scoped — they load only when Claude works with files matching specific glob patterns, keeping context lean.

Directory Structure

All .md files within .claude/rules/ are discovered recursively, so you can organize them in subdirectories.

your-project/
  .claude/
    CLAUDE.md
    rules/
      code-style.md          # Unconditional (no paths)
      testing.md              # Unconditional
      security.md             # Unconditional
      frontend/
        react-patterns.md     # Path-triggered
        css-guidelines.md     # Path-triggered

Unconditional vs Path-Triggered Rules

TypeFrontmatterLoaded When
UnconditionalNo paths field (or absent frontmatter)At launch — same priority as .claude/CLAUDE.md
Path-triggeredHas paths YAML frontmatterWhen Claude reads files matching the glob pattern

Path-Triggered Rules with YAML Frontmatter

---
paths:
  - "src/api/**/*.ts"
---

# API Development Rules
- All API endpoints must include input validation
- Use Zod schemas for request/response types
- Include error handling middleware

Glob patterns follow standard conventions:

PatternMatches
**/*.tsAll TypeScript files in any directory
src/**/*All files under src/
*.mdMarkdown files in project root only
src/components/*.tsxReact components in specific directory
src/**/*.{ts,tsx}Brace expansion for multiple extensions

User-Level Rules

Personal rules at ~/.claude/rules/ apply to every project. They are loaded before project rules, meaning project rules have higher priority and can override user-level guidance when both apply to the same concern.

~/.claude/
  rules/
    my-code-style.md     # Your personal style preferences
    git-workflow.md      # Your personal git habits

Priority and Loading Behavior

  • User rules (~/.claude/rules/) load first
  • Project rules (.claude/rules/) load with higher priority
  • Rules without paths load at the same priority as .claude/CLAUDE.md
  • Path-triggered rules load on demand when matching files enter context
  • Symlinks are supported; circular symlinks are detected gracefully
When to Use Rules vs CLAUDE.md

CLAUDE.md is best for project-wide facts that always apply: build commands, architecture overview, team conventions. Rules are best for domain-specific guidance scoped to file types or directories: API patterns for src/api/, React patterns for *.tsx, database conventions for migrations/. This keeps context lean — path-triggered rules only load when relevant.

Exam-Critical: Loading Distinction

Rules without paths frontmatter load unconditionally at launch. Rules with paths frontmatter load only when Claude reads files matching the pattern. This distinction is a common exam question.
summary: The .claude/rules/ directory organizes modular project instructions as markdown files. Rules without a paths frontmatter field load unconditionally at launch. Rules with paths frontmatter load only when Claude reads files matching the glob patterns. User-level rules at ~/.claude/rules/ apply to all projects but have lower priority than project rules. Files are discovered recursively, supporting subdirectories and symlinks.

key_concepts.definition: Rules without a paths field in YAML frontmatter. Loaded at launch alongside .claude/CLAUDE.md.
Rules with a paths field containing glob patterns. Loaded on demand only when Claude reads files matching those patterns.
Rules at ~/.claude/rules/ that apply to every project. Loaded before project rules (lower priority).
All .md files within .claude/rules/ are discovered recursively, including subdirectories. Symlinks supported.

title: Skills System
content:
Skills: On-Demand Instructions and Workflows

Skills are the primary mechanism for packaging reusable instructions, scripts, and resources that Claude loads only when relevant. Unlike CLAUDE.md (loaded every session), skills use progressive disclosure to minimize context cost until actually needed. Skills follow the Agent Skills open standard and extend it with Claude Code-specific features.

Progressive Disclosure (Three-Tier Loading)

TierWhat LoadsContext CostWhen
MetadataName + description only~100 tokensAt startup, for all skills
InstructionsFull SKILL.md body<5,000 tokens recommendedWhen skill is invoked
ResourcesFiles in scripts/, references/, assets/As neededDuring execution

Directory Structure

my-skill/
  SKILL.md           # Main instructions (required)
  template.md        # Optional template
  examples/
    sample.md        # Optional example output
  scripts/
    validate.sh      # Optional script

Commands-to-Skills Merger

The legacy .claude/commands/ system has been merged into skills. Both .claude/commands/deploy.md and .claude/skills/deploy/SKILL.md create the /deploy command. Existing command files keep working, but if a skill and command share the same name, the skill takes precedence. New work should use skills for the additional capabilities: directory for supporting files, frontmatter for invocation control, and auto-invocation by Claude.

Skill Locations (Priority Order)

LocationPathPriority
Enterprise (managed)Managed settingsHighest
Personal~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.mdMedium
Project.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.mdLower
Plugin<plugin>/skills/<name>/SKILL.mdNamespaced (plugin:skill)

All SKILL.md Frontmatter Fields

---
name: my-skill                    # Display name (lowercase, hyphens, max 64 chars)
description: What this skill does  # Recommended; truncated at 1,536 chars with when_to_use
when_to_use: Additional triggers   # Appended to description in listing
argument-hint: "[issue-number]"    # Shown during autocomplete
disable-model-invocation: true     # Only user can invoke (removes from context)
user-invocable: true               # false = hidden from / menu
allowed-tools: Read Grep           # Pre-approved tools (space-separated or YAML list)
model: sonnet                      # Model override when active
effort: high                       # low|medium|high|xhigh|max
context: fork                      # Run in forked subagent
agent: Explore                     # Subagent type for context:fork
hooks:                             # Hooks scoped to skill lifecycle
  PreToolUse:
    - matcher: "Bash"
      hooks:
        - type: command
          command: "./scripts/check.sh"
          once: true
paths: "src/**/*.ts"               # Glob patterns limiting auto-activation
shell: bash                        # bash (default) or powershell
---
14+ Frontmatter Fields

Additional open standard fields include license, compatibility (max 500 chars), and metadata (arbitrary key-value mapping). Claude Code extensions beyond the standard: when_to_use, argument-hint, disable-model-invocation, user-invocable, model, effort, context, agent, hooks, paths, shell.

String Substitutions

VariableDescription
$ARGUMENTSAll arguments passed when invoking
$ARGUMENTS[N] / $NSpecific argument by 0-based index (shell-style quoting)
${CLAUDE_SESSION_ID}Current session ID
${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}Directory containing SKILL.md

If $ARGUMENTS is not present in skill content, arguments are appended as ARGUMENTS: <value>.

Dynamic Context Injection

The !`command` syntax runs shell commands before skill content is sent to Claude. Output replaces the placeholder:

## Pull request context
- PR diff: !`gh pr diff`
- Changed files: !`gh pr diff --name-only`

Disable with "disableSkillShellExecution": true in settings.

Invocation Control Matrix

FrontmatterUser InvokesClaude InvokesDescription in Context
(default)YesYesAlways
disable-model-invocation: trueYesNoNOT in context
user-invocable: falseNo (hidden from menu)YesAlways

context:fork and the agent Field

Setting context: fork runs the skill in an isolated subagent. The skill content becomes the subagent's prompt; it has no access to conversation history. The agent field specifies the subagent type: Explore, Plan, general-purpose, or a custom agent name.

Compaction Budget

After compaction, skills are re-attached: first 5,000 tokens of each skill, combined budget of 25,000 tokens across all re-attached skills. Budget fills from most recently invoked skill; older skills may be dropped entirely.

Description Budget

Skill descriptions stay in context so Claude knows what's available. Budget scales at 1% of context window with a fallback of 8,000 characters. Each entry's combined description + when_to_use is capped at 1,536 characters. Override with SLASH_COMMAND_TOOL_CHAR_BUDGET env var.

Extended Thinking

Include the word "ultrathink" anywhere in skill content to enable extended thinking for that skill's execution.
summary: Skills package reusable instructions with progressive disclosure: metadata (~100 tokens) at startup, full SKILL.md on invocation, resources as needed. Skills have 14+ frontmatter fields including invocation control (disable-model-invocation, user-invocable), context:fork for subagent isolation, and dynamic shell injection with !`command`. The commands-to-skills merger means both .claude/commands/ and .claude/skills/ create slash commands, with skills taking precedence. Description budget is 1% of context window (8K fallback), per-entry cap of 1,536 chars. Compaction re-attaches 5K tokens per skill, 25K total.

key_concepts.definition: Three-tier loading: metadata (~100 tokens) at startup, instructions (<5K tokens) on invocation, resources as needed. Minimizes context cost.
When true, only users can invoke the skill (via /name). Removes description from context entirely, preventing Claude from auto-invoking.
Runs skill in an isolated subagent with no conversation history access. Skill content becomes the subagent's prompt.
.claude/commands/ and .claude/skills/ both create slash commands. If both exist with the same name, the skill takes precedence.
Combined description + when_to_use text is truncated at 1,536 characters per skill in the listing context.
After compaction: 5,000 tokens per skill, 25,000 tokens combined. Filled from most recently invoked skill.
The !`command` syntax runs shell commands before skill content reaches Claude. Output replaces the placeholder.

title: Plan Mode and Permission Modes
content:
Permission Modes: Controlling Claude's Autonomy

Claude Code provides six permission modes that govern what actions Claude can take without explicit approval. Understanding these modes, their evaluation order, and how to configure them is critical for both security and productivity.

The Six Permission Modes

ModeAuto-ApprovesBest For
defaultReads onlyGetting started, sensitive work
acceptEditsReads, file edits, common filesystem commands (mkdir, touch, mv, cp, rm, rmdir, sed)Iterating on code
planReads only (no edits at all)Exploring before changing
autoEverything, with background safety classifierLong tasks, reducing prompt fatigue
dontAskOnly pre-approved tools via allow rulesLocked-down CI/scripts
bypassPermissionsEverything except protected pathsIsolated containers/VMs only

Plan Mode Deep Dive

Plan mode uses a read-only subagent that inherits the main conversation's model. Write and Edit tools are denied. The subagent gathers context, researches the codebase, and presents a plan.

  • Activate via CLI: --permission-mode plan
  • In-session: /plan [description]
  • Shift+Tab cycling through modes
  • Ctrl+G opens the plan in your text editor for direct editing
  • After plan review: approve with auto mode, accept edits, manual review, keep planning, or refine with Ultraplan

Auto Mode Classifier

Auto mode uses a separate server-configured model as a safety classifier. It evaluates each action Claude proposes against block rules.

Classifier BlocksClassifier Allows
curl | bash, sending creds externally, production deploys, mass deletion, IAM modifications, force push to mainLocal file ops in working dir, dependency installs, reading .env and sending to matching API, read-only HTTP, pushing to current/new branch

After 3 consecutive blocks or 20 total blocks in a session, auto mode falls back. In non-interactive mode, repeated blocks abort the session.

Permission Rule Syntax

Rules use the format Tool or Tool(specifier):

{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": [
      "Read",
      "Bash(git log *)",
      "Bash(npm test *)",
      "mcp__github__*"
    ],
    "deny": [
      "Bash(rm -rf *)",
      "Agent(Explore)"
    ]
  }
}
SyntaxMeaning
Bash(*)Equivalent to Bash (matches all)
Bash(ls *)Space before * enforces word boundary: matches ls -la but NOT lsof
Bash(ls:*):* suffix equivalent to trailing wildcard
mcp__servername__*All tools from an MCP server
Agent(AgentName)Control subagent usage

Permission Evaluation Order

The evaluation follows a strict precedence: deny > ask > allow. First match wins, and deny always takes precedence regardless of order.

  1. Hooks — can allow, deny, or continue
  2. Deny rules — if match, BLOCKED (even in bypassPermissions)
  3. Permission mode — bypassPermissions approves, acceptEdits approves file ops, others fall through
  4. Allow rules — if match, APPROVED
  5. canUseTool callback (SDK) or user prompt

Protected Paths (All Modes)

Certain paths are protected across all permission modes:

  • Directories: .git, .vscode, .idea, .husky, .claude (except .claude/commands, .claude/agents, .claude/skills, .claude/worktrees)
  • Files: .gitconfig, .bashrc, .zshrc, .mcp.json, .claude.json, and other shell/git configs

Shift+Tab Mode Cycling

Press Shift+Tab to cycle: default → acceptEdits → plan → auto. The --allow-dangerously-skip-permissions flag adds bypassPermissions to the cycle.

Exam-Critical: Evaluation Order

Permission precedence is deny > ask > allow. A deny rule always wins over an allow rule, even if the allow rule is more specific. Hooks evaluate first and can override the entire chain.

Decision Guide: Planning Mode vs Direct Execution
Are 45+ files affected or multiple architectural approaches possible?
Yes →
Use Planning Mode (Shift+Tab twice)
No →
Is there a clear stack trace pointing to a single file?
Yes →
Use Direct Execution
No →
Does the change span multiple concerns?
Yes →
Use Planning Mode
No →
Use Direct Execution

skilljar_refs.lesson: Permissions, Usage, and Choosing Your Model

summary: Claude Code provides six permission modes: default (reads only), acceptEdits (reads + file edits + filesystem commands), plan (read-only, no edits), auto (classifier-based approval), dontAsk (only pre-approved tools), and bypassPermissions (everything except protected paths). Plan mode uses a read-only subagent with Ctrl+G for editing plans. Permission evaluation order is deny > ask > allow, with hooks evaluating first. Auto mode uses a background classifier that blocks dangerous operations and falls back after 3 consecutive or 20 total blocks.

key_concepts.definition: default, acceptEdits, plan, auto, dontAsk, bypassPermissions  --  each with different auto-approval behavior for tools and actions.
Read-only exploration mode using a subagent. Write/Edit tools denied. Ctrl+G opens plan in text editor.
Separate server-configured model evaluating each action. Falls back after 3 consecutive or 20 total blocks.
deny > ask > allow. Hooks evaluate first, then deny rules, then permission mode, then allow rules, then callback/prompt.
Paths like .git, .vscode, .claude (except commands/agents/skills/worktrees) are protected across all permission modes.
Format: Tool or Tool(specifier). Wildcards with *, word boundary enforcement with space before *, :* suffix for trailing wildcard.

title: CLI Flags and Session Management
content:
Claude Code CLI: Commands, Flags, and Session Control

The Claude Code CLI is the primary interface for both interactive development and headless automation. Understanding the flag categories, print-mode-only restrictions, system prompt flags, and session management is essential for effective use in both development and CI/CD contexts.

Core Commands

CommandDescription
claudeStart interactive session
claude "query"Start with initial prompt
claude -p "query"Print mode (non-interactive), then exit
cat file | claude -p "query"Process piped content
claude -cContinue most recent conversation
claude -r "session" "query"Resume by ID or name
claude updateUpdate to latest version
claude auth loginSign in (--email, --sso, --console)
claude agentsList all configured subagents
claude mcpConfigure MCP servers
claude auto-mode defaultsPrint built-in auto mode classifier rules as JSON

Print Mode Flags (Non-Interactive Only)

These flags only work with -p (print mode):

FlagDescriptionExample
--output-formatOutput format: text, json, stream-json--output-format json
--max-turnsLimit agentic turns (exits with error at limit)--max-turns 3
--max-budget-usdDollar limit for API calls--max-budget-usd 5.00
--json-schemaValidated JSON output matching schema--json-schema '{...}'
--no-session-persistenceDon't save sessions to disk—
--fallback-modelFallback when primary overloaded—
Exam-Critical: Print-Mode-Only Flags

--max-turns, --max-budget-usd, --json-schema, --no-session-persistence, and --fallback-model are only valid in print mode (-p). Using them in interactive mode will not work as expected.

Session Management Flags

FlagDescription
--continue / -cLoad most recent conversation in current directory
--resume / -rResume session by ID or name
--fork-sessionCreate new session ID when resuming (preserves history, original unchanged)
--name / -nDisplay name for session
--from-prResume sessions linked to GitHub PR

System Prompt Flags

FlagBehavior
--system-promptReplaces entire default prompt
--system-prompt-fileReplaces with file contents
--append-system-promptAppends to default prompt
--append-system-prompt-fileAppends file contents to default prompt

--system-prompt and --system-prompt-file are mutually exclusive. Append flags can be combined with either replacement flag.

Tool Restriction

# Restrict to specific tools only
claude -p --tools "Read,Grep,Glob" "analyze the codebase"

# Allow tools without permission prompt
claude --allowedTools "Bash(git log *)" "Read"

# Remove tools from context entirely
claude --disallowedTools "WebSearch" "WebFetch"

--bare Mode

--bare provides minimal startup by skipping: hooks, skills, plugins, MCP servers, auto memory, and CLAUDE.md. Only Bash, Read, and Edit tools are available. Auth must come from ANTHROPIC_API_KEY or apiKeyHelper. Useful for CI/scripts where startup speed matters.

Other Important Flags

FlagDescription
--worktree / -wStart in isolated git worktree at .claude/worktrees/<name>
--agentSpecify agent for session (main thread runs as named agent)
--mcp-configLoad MCP servers from JSON file (combinable with --strict-mcp-config)
--add-dirAdditional working directories
--permission-modeSet mode: default, acceptEdits, plan, auto, dontAsk, bypassPermissions
--effortEffort level: low, medium, high, xhigh, max
--modelSet model by alias (sonnet, opus) or full name
Decision Guide: New Session vs Resume
Are previous results potentially stale (code changed, time passed)?
Yes →
Start new session
No →
Is the context window still fresh and relevant?
Yes →
Resume session (--resume flag)
No →
Would resuming load outdated context?
Yes →
Start new session
No →
Resume session
summary: The Claude Code CLI supports core commands (claude, claude -p, claude -c, claude -r), print-mode-only flags (--max-turns, --max-budget-usd, --json-schema, --output-format), session management (--continue, --resume, --fork-session, --name), and system prompt customization (--system-prompt replaces, --append-system-prompt appends; mutually exclusive replacement flags). --bare mode skips hooks, skills, plugins, MCP, auto memory, and CLAUDE.md for faster startup. --worktree creates isolated git worktrees.

key_concepts.definition: Non-interactive mode for scripting and CI. Required for --max-turns, --max-budget-usd, --json-schema, --output-format, --fallback-model.
--system-prompt REPLACES the entire default prompt. --append-system-prompt APPENDS to it. Replace flags are mutually exclusive.
Minimal startup: skips hooks, skills, plugins, MCP, auto memory, CLAUDE.md. Only Bash/Read/Edit available. Good for CI.
--continue (most recent), --resume (by ID/name), --fork-session (new ID preserving history), --name (display name).
Creates isolated git worktree at .claude/worktrees/<name> with branch worktree-<name> based on origin/HEAD.
text (default plain text), json (structured after completion), stream-json (newline-delimited streaming events).

title: CI/CD and GitHub Actions Integration
content:
Automating Claude Code in CI/CD Pipelines

Claude Code integrates with GitHub Actions via anthropics/claude-code-action@v1 for automated code review, PR management, and scheduled cloud tasks. This topic covers the action configuration, trigger mechanisms, CLI args passthrough, cloud providers, and routines.

claude-code-action@v1 Setup

Two setup paths:

  • Quick: Run /install-github-app in Claude Code terminal
  • Manual: Install Claude GitHub App → add ANTHROPIC_API_KEY to repository secrets → copy workflow file
name: Claude Code
on:
  issue_comment:
    types: [created]
  pull_request_review_comment:
    types: [created]
jobs:
  claude:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          anthropic_api_key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}

The @claude Trigger

Claude responds to @claude mentions in issue comments, PR comments, and PR review comments. This is not /claude — it uses the @ prefix.

@claude implement this feature based on the issue description
@claude fix the TypeError in the user dashboard component

Custom triggers: use the trigger_phrase parameter to change from the default @claude.

Action Parameters (v1)

ParameterDescriptionRequired
promptInstructions for Claude (text or skill name)No
claude_argsCLI arguments passed through to Claude CodeNo
anthropic_api_keyClaude API keyYes (direct API)
github_tokenGitHub token for API accessNo
trigger_phraseCustom trigger (default: @claude)No
use_bedrockUse AWS BedrockNo
use_vertexUse Google Vertex AINo

CLI Args Passthrough

Pass any Claude Code CLI flags through the claude_args parameter:

claude_args: "--max-turns 5 --model claude-sonnet-4-6 --append-system-prompt 'Follow our standards'"

Common flags for CI: --max-turns (limit turns, default 10 in action), --model, --mcp-config, --allowedTools, --append-system-prompt.

Cloud Provider Support

ProviderAuth MethodModel Format
AWS BedrockOIDC authenticationus.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-6
Google Vertex AIWorkload Identity Federationclaude-sonnet-4-5@20250929

Both providers recommend a custom GitHub App for best security practices.

Routines (Scheduled Cloud Tasks)

Routines are saved Claude Code configurations that run on Anthropic-managed cloud infrastructure. Manage at claude.ai/code/routines or via /schedule in the CLI.

Trigger TypeDescription
ScheduledHourly, daily, weekdays, weekly; custom cron via /schedule update (min 1 hour)
APIHTTP POST to per-routine endpoint with bearer token; returns session ID and URL
GitHubReact to pull_request and release events with filters (author, title, labels, etc.)

Routines clone repos on every run from the default branch. Claude creates claude/-prefixed branches by default. Each GitHub event match starts a new session (no session reuse).

Routine Use Cases
  • Backlog maintenance: nightly issue triage, labeling, and Slack summary
  • Alert triage: monitoring calls API endpoint on error threshold; Claude correlates with recent commits
  • Bespoke code review: GitHub trigger on PR opened; applies team review checklist
  • Deploy verification: CD pipeline calls endpoint after deploy; Claude runs smoke checks
  • Docs drift: weekly scan of merged PRs, flags outdated docs, opens update PRs
Exam-Critical: @claude Trigger

GitHub Actions integration uses @claude (at-sign), not /claude (slash). The trigger works in issue comments, PR comments, and PR review comments.
summary: Claude Code integrates with GitHub Actions via anthropics/claude-code-action@v1. The @claude trigger (not /claude) works in issue and PR comments. Key action parameters: prompt, claude_args (CLI passthrough), anthropic_api_key, trigger_phrase. Cloud providers: AWS Bedrock (OIDC) and Google Vertex AI (WIF). Routines provide scheduled cloud tasks with three trigger types: scheduled (cron), API (HTTP POST), and GitHub events (PR, releases with filters).

key_concepts.definition: Official GitHub Action for integrating Claude Code into CI/CD workflows. Parameters include prompt, claude_args, and cloud provider flags.
Mention @claude in issue/PR comments to trigger Claude. Uses @ prefix (not /). Customizable via trigger_phrase parameter.
The claude_args parameter passes any CLI flags to Claude Code, enabling --max-turns, --model, --append-system-prompt in CI.
Saved Claude Code configurations running on Anthropic cloud. Three triggers: scheduled (cron, min 1hr), API (HTTP POST with bearer token), GitHub events.
AWS Bedrock (OIDC auth, model: us.anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-6) and Google Vertex AI (WIF, model: claude-sonnet-4-5@20250929).

title: Claude Agent SDK
content:
Claude Agent SDK: Programmatic Claude Code

The Claude Agent SDK (formerly Claude Code SDK) gives you the same tools, agent loop, and context management that power Claude Code, programmable in Python and TypeScript. It is the programmatic interface for building custom agents that read files, run commands, search the web, and edit code autonomously.

SDK vs Client SDK vs CLI

InterfaceBehaviorUse Case
Client SDKYou implement the tool loop manuallyFull control over every API call
Agent SDKClaude handles tools autonomously (built-in tool execution)CI/CD, custom apps, production automation
CLIInteractive terminal interfaceDevelopment, one-off tasks

Installation

# TypeScript
npm install @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk

# Python
pip install claude-agent-sdk

The query() Entry Point

query() is the main entry point that creates the agentic loop and returns an async iterator:

# Python
from claude_agent_sdk import query, ClaudeAgentOptions

async for message in query(
    prompt="Find and fix the bug in auth.py",
    options=ClaudeAgentOptions(
        allowed_tools=["Read", "Edit", "Bash"],
        permission_mode="acceptEdits",
        max_turns=10,
    ),
):
    print(message)
// TypeScript
import { query } from "@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk";

for await (const message of query({
  prompt: "Find and fix the bug in auth.ts",
  options: {
    allowedTools: ["Read", "Edit", "Bash"],
    permissionMode: "acceptEdits",
    maxTurns: 10,
  }
})) {
  console.log(message);
}

Message Types (5 Core)

TypeDescription
SystemMessageSession lifecycle: init (first message) and compact_boundary (after compaction)
AssistantMessageClaude's response with text and/or tool call blocks
UserMessageAfter tool execution with results, or user inputs
StreamEventRaw API streaming events (only with partial messages enabled)
ResultMessageEnd of agent loop with result, cost, usage, session_id

ResultMessage Subtypes

SubtypeMeaningHas result?
successFinished normallyYes
error_max_turnsHit maxTurns limitNo
error_max_budget_usdHit maxBudgetUsd limitNo
error_during_executionError interrupted loopNo
error_max_structured_output_retriesSchema validation failed after retriesNo

All subtypes carry total_cost_usd, usage, num_turns, session_id.

Custom Tools

Custom tools use the SDK's in-process MCP server — not a separate process:

# Python
from claude_agent_sdk import tool, create_sdk_mcp_server

@tool("get_weather", "Get weather for a city", {"city": str})
async def get_weather(args: dict) -> dict:
    return {"content": [{"type": "text", "text": f"72F in {args['city']}"}]}

server = create_sdk_mcp_server(
    name="weather", version="1.0.0", tools=[get_weather]
)

Tool Naming Convention

MCP tools follow the pattern: mcp__<server_name>__<tool_name>. Wildcard: mcp__weather__* covers all tools from the server.

Permission Modes in SDK

ModeBehaviorAvailable
defaultRequires canUseTool callback; no callback = denyBoth
acceptEditsAuto-approves file edits + filesystem commandsBoth
planNo tool execution; plan onlyBoth
dontAskPre-approved only; everything else deniedBoth
autoModel classifier approves/deniesTypeScript only
bypassPermissionsAll tools without promptsBoth

Programmatic vs Filesystem Hooks

TypeDefined InScope
Filesystem hookssettings.json (command, http, prompt, agent)Main agent AND subagents
Programmatic hooksquery() options callbacksMain session only

settingSources

Controls which filesystem-based settings the SDK loads. Default: ["user", "project", "local"]. Pass [] to disable all filesystem settings.

Session Management

  • Python: ClaudeSDKClient class manages sessions; client.query() automatically continues
  • TypeScript: continue: true on subsequent query() calls
  • Resume: Capture session_id from ResultMessage, pass to resume option
  • Fork: fork_session=True creates new session with copy of history

Python vs TypeScript Key Differences

FeaturePythonTypeScript
Packageclaude-agent-sdk@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk
Field namingsnake_casecamelCase
auto permission modeNot availableAvailable
Default effortUnset (model default)"high"
env in optionsMerged on top of inheritedReplaces inherited (must spread process.env)
Session clientClaudeSDKClient classcontinue: true on query
Hook events10 events18 events (includes SessionStart/End, etc.)
Content accessmessage.contentmessage.message.content (wrapped)
Type checkingisinstance(msg, ResultMessage)msg.type === "result"
Exam-Critical: SDK Differences

Python and TypeScript SDKs differ in important ways: auto permission mode is TypeScript-only, Python's env merges while TypeScript's replaces, default effort differs, and TypeScript supports 18 hook events vs Python's 10.
summary: The Claude Agent SDK provides programmatic access to Claude Code's agent loop via query() in Python and TypeScript. It yields 5 message types (System, Assistant, User, StreamEvent, Result) with ResultMessage subtypes indicating success or specific errors. Custom tools use in-process MCP servers via @tool decorator and create_sdk_mcp_server(). Key Python/TS differences: auto mode is TS-only, env merging behavior differs, Python has 10 hook events vs TS 18, and content access paths differ.

key_concepts.definition: Main SDK function creating the agentic loop. Returns async iterator yielding messages. Parameters: prompt, options (tools, permissions, budget, model).
success, error_max_turns, error_max_budget_usd, error_during_execution, error_max_structured_output_retries. All carry cost and usage data.
Define tools with @tool decorator, register via in-process MCP server. Tool names follow mcp__<server>__<tool> convention.
Controls filesystem settings loading: ['user', 'project', 'local'] by default. Pass [] to disable all. Managed policy always loads regardless.
Filesystem hooks (settings.json) fire in main agent AND subagents. Programmatic hooks (query() callbacks) are scoped to main session only.
auto mode TS-only, env merges (Python) vs replaces (TS), 10 vs 18 hook events, snake_case vs camelCase, different session management patterns.

title: Settings System and .claude Directory
content:
Settings Precedence and the .claude Directory

Claude Code's settings system uses a layered precedence model where higher-priority sources override lower ones for scalar values, while array values are merged. Understanding this system — especially managed settings delivery and the .claude directory structure — is essential for enterprise configuration and team workflows.

Settings Precedence (Highest to Lowest)

PrioritySourceLocationCan Override?
1 (highest)ManagedServer / MDM / file-basedCannot be overridden by any lower layer
2CLI argumentsCommand-line flagsTemporary session overrides
3Local.claude/settings.local.jsonGitignored; personal project settings
4Project.claude/settings.jsonShared via source control
5 (lowest)User~/.claude/settings.jsonGlobal personal settings

Managed Settings Delivery

Managed settings are delivered through three mechanisms, with their own internal precedence:

  1. Server-managed: Via Anthropic servers through Claude.ai admin console (highest)
  2. MDM/OS-level:
    • macOS: com.anthropic.claudecode managed preferences domain
    • Windows: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\ClaudeCode registry (REG_SZ JSON)
    • Windows user-level: HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\ClaudeCode (lowest policy priority)
  3. File-based:
    • macOS: /Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/managed-settings.json
    • Linux/WSL: /etc/claude-code/managed-settings.json
    • Windows: C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\managed-settings.json
    • Drop-in directory: managed-settings.d/ alongside the file (alphabetical merge)

Array Merge vs Scalar Override

Settings behavior differs by value type:

  • Scalar settings (strings, numbers, booleans): most-specific value wins (higher priority overrides lower)
  • Array settings (permissions.allow, sandbox.filesystem.allowWrite, etc.): concatenate and deduplicate across all scopes

This means permission allow rules from all layers combine additively — a user-level allow rule is never overridden by a project setting, they merge.

.claude Directory Structure

PathPurposeCommitted?
.claude/settings.jsonPermissions, hooks, configurationYes
.claude/settings.local.jsonPersonal settings overridesGitignored
.claude/CLAUDE.mdProject instructions (alternative to ./CLAUDE.md)Yes
.claude/rules/Topic-scoped instructionsYes
.claude/skills/Reusable prompts and workflowsYes
.claude/commands/Single-file prompts (legacy; use skills/)Yes
.claude/agents/Custom subagent definitionsYes
.claude/output-styles/Custom output stylesYes
.claude/worktrees/Git worktree directoriesGitignored

User-Level Files (~/.claude/)

PathPurpose
~/.claude/CLAUDE.mdGlobal instructions for all sessions
~/.claude/settings.jsonUser-wide settings
~/.claude/agents/User-wide subagent definitions
~/.claude/skills/User-wide skills
~/.claude/rules/User-wide rules (lower priority than project rules)
~/.claude/plans/Plan files from plan mode
~/.claude/projects/Session data (plaintext JSONL)
~/.claude.jsonPreferences, OAuth, MCP servers, per-project state

Global Config (~/.claude.json)

The global config file stores preferences that apply across all sessions:

  • editorMode: "normal" or "vim"
  • teammateMode: "auto", "in-process", "tmux"
  • autoConnectIde, autoInstallIdeExtension
  • MCP server configurations (local scope)
  • OAuth tokens and per-project state

Sandbox Settings

Sandbox provides OS-level isolation for Bash commands:

SettingDescription
sandbox.enabledEnable bash sandboxing (macOS, Linux, WSL2)
sandbox.failIfUnavailableExit at startup if sandbox unavailable
sandbox.autoAllowBashIfSandboxedAuto-approve bash when sandboxed (default: true)
sandbox.filesystem.allowWriteAdditional writable paths (merged across scopes)
sandbox.filesystem.denyWritePaths denied writes
sandbox.filesystem.denyReadPaths denied reads
sandbox.network.allowedDomainsAllowed outbound domains

Verification

Use /status to show active settings sources and origins. Use /config to open the tabbed Settings interface. JSON schema validation: "$schema": "https://json.schemastore.org/claude-code-settings.json".

Exam-Critical: Precedence Order

Settings precedence is Managed > CLI > Local > Project > User. Managed settings cannot be overridden. Array settings (permissions, sandbox paths) merge across all layers; scalar settings use the highest-priority value.
summary: Claude Code settings follow a five-layer precedence: Managed (highest, cannot be overridden) > CLI arguments > Local (.claude/settings.local.json) > Project (.claude/settings.json) > User (~/.claude/settings.json). Managed settings are delivered via server, MDM/OS-level (macOS plist, Windows registry), or file-based paths. Array settings merge across scopes; scalar settings use the highest-priority value. The .claude directory contains settings, rules, skills, agents, and worktrees.

key_concepts.definition: Managed > CLI > Local > Project > User. Managed cannot be overridden. CLI provides temporary session overrides.
Three mechanisms: server-managed (admin console), MDM/OS-level (macOS plist, Windows registry), file-based (managed-settings.json with drop-in .d/ directory).
Array settings (permissions, sandbox paths) concatenate and deduplicate across scopes. Scalar settings use the most-specific value.
Contains settings.json (committed), settings.local.json (gitignored), rules/, skills/, agents/, commands/, output-styles/, worktrees/ (gitignored).
OS-level isolation for Bash: configurable filesystem read/write/deny paths, network domain allowlists. autoAllowBashIfSandboxed defaults to true.
Stores OAuth tokens, MCP server configs (local scope), editor preferences, per-project state. Separate from settings.json.

title: Prompting Best Practices
content:
Prompting Best Practices

Effective prompting is the foundation of every Claude integration. Think of Claude as a "brilliant but new employee who lacks context on your norms and workflows." The golden rule: show your prompt to a colleague with minimal context -- if they would be confused, Claude will be too.

Be Clear and Direct

Provide instructions as sequential steps using numbered lists or bullet points when order and completeness matter. If you want Claude to go above and beyond, explicitly request it rather than relying on inference.

Create an analytics dashboard. Include as many relevant features and interactions
as possible. Go beyond the basics to create a fully-featured implementation.

Tell Claude what to do instead of what not to do: "Your response should be composed of smoothly flowing prose paragraphs" is better than "Do not use markdown."

Add Context (Explain Why)

Providing motivation behind instructions helps Claude generalize. Instead of bare rules, explain the reasoning:

Bad:  "NEVER use ellipses."
Good: "Your response will be read aloud by a text-to-speech engine,
       so never use ellipses since the TTS engine will not know
       how to pronounce them."

Claude can generalize from the explanation -- understanding why a rule exists lets the model apply the principle to situations you did not explicitly cover.

XML Tags for Structure

XML tags help Claude parse complex prompts unambiguously, especially when mixing instructions, context, examples, and variable inputs. Use consistent, descriptive tag names and nest tags for natural hierarchy.

<documents>
  <document index="1">
    <source>annual_report_2023.pdf</source>
    <document_content>
      {{ANNUAL_REPORT}}
    </document_content>
  </document>
</documents>

Common tags include <documents>, <example>/<examples>, <input>, <instructions>, and <context>.

Role Prompting in System Prompt

Set a role in the system prompt to focus behavior and tone. Even a single sentence makes a measurable difference:

message = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-7",
    max_tokens=1024,
    system="You are a helpful coding assistant specializing in Python.",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "How do I sort a list of dicts by key?"}],
)

Long Context: Data at Top, Query at Bottom

For prompts exceeding 20k tokens, place longform data (documents, logs, records) at the top of the prompt, with your query and instructions at the bottom. This ordering can improve response quality by up to 30% in tests with complex multi-document inputs.

Structure documents with XML tags: wrap each in <document> with <document_content> and <source> subtags for clear attribution.

Ground in Quotes Before Analysis

For long document tasks, instruct Claude to extract relevant quotes before performing its analysis. This "quote-then-analyze" pattern cuts through noise and reduces hallucination:

Find quotes from the patient records relevant to the reported symptoms.
Place these in <quotes> tags. Then, based on these quotes, list diagnostic
information in <info> tags.

Prefill Deprecation (Migration for 4.6+)

Starting with Claude 4.6, prefilled responses on the last assistant turn are no longer supported. On Mythos Preview, prefilled assistant messages return a 400 error.

Common Misconception

If you were using prefill for output formatting (e.g., starting with { for JSON), you must migrate to output_config.format or structured outputs. Other prefill use cases have specific migration paths:

Old Prefill Use CaseNew Approach
Output formatting (JSON, YAML)Structured Outputs (output_config.format) or tools with enum fields
Eliminating preamblesSystem prompt: "Respond directly without preamble." Or XML tags / structured outputs
Avoiding bad refusalsClear prompting within the user message (Claude 4.6+ is much better at appropriate refusals)
ContinuationsMove to user message: "Your previous response ended with [text]. Continue from there."
Context hydrationInject reminders into user turn, or hydrate via tools

Format Control

Four techniques for controlling output format, in order of reliability:

  1. Structured Outputs (output_config.format) -- guarantees valid JSON matching a schema
  2. XML format indicators -- wrap expected output in descriptive tags like <smoothly_flowing_prose_paragraphs>
  3. Positive instructions -- "Write in flowing prose" beats "Don't use markdown"
  4. Match prompt style -- your prompt's formatting style influences Claude's response formatting
Exam-Critical Facts
  • Long context: data at top, query at bottom = up to 30% improvement
  • Prefill is deprecated on Claude 4.6+, returns 400 on Mythos Preview
  • Role prompting goes in the system parameter, not the user message
  • XML tags (<documents>, <example>, <input>) reduce misinterpretation in complex prompts
  • "Quote first, then analyze" reduces hallucination in long-document tasks
Common Anti-Patterns
  • Anti-pattern: Vague instructions like "be more conservative" or "try harder" without explicit criteria
    Instead: Provide concrete thresholds and criteria: "Report any bugs that could cause incorrect behavior, a test failure, or a misleading result; only omit pure style nits"
  • Anti-pattern: Retry loops for information that does not exist in the source document
    Instead: Instruct Claude to report "not found" explicitly rather than fabricating an answer; set a maximum retry count and accept absence as a valid result
  • Anti-pattern: Self-review in the same instance (confirmation bias -- the same biases that produced the output persist in self-review)
    Instead: Use separate API calls for Generate, Review, and Refine (independent context); or use the finding pass + filtering pass pattern with distinct prompts
summary: Core prompting techniques: be clear and direct, add context by explaining why, use XML tags for structure, set roles in system prompts, place data at top with query at bottom for long contexts (30% improvement), ground responses in quotes before analysis, migrate away from prefill (deprecated on 4.6+), and control format with structured outputs or positive instructions.

key_concepts.definition: Provide explicit, sequential instructions. Think of Claude as a brilliant new employee who needs full context on your norms.
Place documents at the top of the prompt, query and instructions at the bottom. This improves quality by up to 30% on complex multi-document inputs.
Structural tags like <documents>, <example>, <input> that help Claude parse complex prompts without ambiguity. Use consistent, descriptive names and nest naturally.
Setting Claude's role in the system prompt parameter to focus behavior, tone, and domain expertise. Even a single sentence is effective.
Prefilled assistant responses are deprecated on Claude 4.6+ and return a 400 error on Mythos Preview. Migrate to structured outputs, clear system prompts, or user-message instructions.
For long document tasks, ask Claude to extract relevant quotes first (in tags), then perform analysis based on those quotes. Reduces hallucination by grounding in source material.

title: Few-Shot and Multishot Prompting
content:
Few-Shot and Multishot Prompting

Examples are one of the most reliable ways to steer Claude's output format, tone, and structure. Few-shot prompting (providing 3-5 examples) is the primary mechanism for disambiguating ambiguous scenarios and demonstrating expected behavior patterns.

3-5 Examples Recommendation

The official Anthropic documentation explicitly recommends 3-5 examples for best results. Fewer than 3 may not provide enough signal for Claude to generalize; more than 5 often yields diminishing returns while consuming context window tokens.

Common Misconception

More examples are not always better. Beyond 5 examples, you consume context window space without proportional improvement. Instead, focus on example quality -- diverse, relevant, and well-structured examples outperform a larger set of similar ones.

Example Quality: Relevant, Diverse, Structured

Each example should meet three criteria:

CriterionWhat It MeansWhy It Matters
RelevantMirror your actual use case closelyClaude generalizes from examples -- irrelevant ones teach wrong patterns
DiverseCover edge cases; vary enough to avoid unintended patternsPrevents Claude from latching onto incidental similarities
StructuredWrapped in proper tags; clear input/output separationHelps Claude distinguish examples from instructions

You can ask Claude to evaluate your examples for relevance and diversity, or to generate additional ones that cover gaps in your set.

Using <example> and <examples> Tags

Always wrap few-shot examples in XML tags so Claude clearly distinguishes them from instructions. The convention is:

<examples>
  <example>
    <input>What is the capital of France?</input>
    <output>The capital of France is Paris.</output>
  </example>
  <example>
    <input>What is the capital of Japan?</input>
    <output>The capital of Japan is Tokyo.</output>
  </example>
  <example>
    <input>What is the capital of Brazil?</input>
    <output>The capital of Brazil is Brasilia (not Rio de Janeiro or Sao Paulo).</output>
  </example>
</examples>

The outer <examples> tag groups all examples; each individual <example> tag contains one input/output pair. This structure prevents Claude from treating example content as literal instructions.

<thinking> Tags in Examples

A powerful technique: include <thinking> tags inside few-shot examples to demonstrate the reasoning pattern you want Claude to follow. Claude will generalize this reasoning style to its own extended thinking blocks.

<example>
  <input>Is 17 a prime number?</input>
  <thinking>
    A prime number is only divisible by 1 and itself.
    Let me check: 17/2 = 8.5, 17/3 = 5.67, 17/4 = 4.25.
    No integer divisors found. 17 is prime.
  </thinking>
  <output>Yes, 17 is a prime number.</output>
</example>

This is especially useful when you want Claude to follow a specific analytical framework, check particular conditions, or reason in a domain-specific way.
