Anti-Hallucination Patterns

Few-shot examples can also teach Claude to resist hallucination. Key techniques:

  • Ground in quotes: Instruct Claude to extract relevant passages from source documents before answering. This forces the model to anchor its reasoning in provided text.
  • Explicit file-reading rule: "Never speculate about code you have not opened. If the user references a specific file, you MUST read the file before answering."
  • Anti-hallucination wrapper: Use an <investigate_before_answering> XML wrapper around instructions that require grounding.
  • Include "I don't know" examples: Show examples where the correct output is an honest admission of uncertainty, so Claude learns that refusing to guess is acceptable.

Practical Example: Classification Task

{
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
  "max_tokens": 256,
  "system": "Classify customer feedback into categories. Use the examples as a guide.",
  "messages": [
    {
      "role": "user",
      "content": "<examples>\n<example>\n<input>The app crashes when I tap settings</input>\n<output>{\"category\": \"bug\", \"severity\": \"high\", \"component\": \"settings\"}</output>\n</example>\n<example>\n<input>Would be nice to have dark mode</input>\n<output>{\"category\": \"feature_request\", \"severity\": \"low\", \"component\": \"ui\"}</output>\n</example>\n<example>\n<input>Love the new dashboard!</input>\n<output>{\"category\": \"positive_feedback\", \"severity\": \"none\", \"component\": \"dashboard\"}</output>\n</example>\n</examples>\n\nClassify this feedback:\n<input>The search feature returns wrong results for product names</input>"
    }
  ]
}
Exam-Critical Facts
  • 3-5 examples is the explicitly recommended range
  • Wrap in <example>/<examples> tags to distinguish from instructions
  • Examples should be relevant, diverse, and structured
  • <thinking> tags in examples teach Claude reasoning patterns that generalize to extended thinking
  • Anti-hallucination: ground in quotes, require file reading before answering, include "I don't know" examples
Decision Guide: Few-shot vs Textual Description
Is the output format ambiguous or complex?
Yes →
Use Few-shot examples (show input→output pairs)
No →
Is the scenario/edge case handling unclear?
Yes →
Use Few-shot examples (demonstrate edge cases)
No →
Use Textual description (general guidance is sufficient)
summary: Few-shot prompting uses 3-5 carefully selected examples to steer Claude's output format, tone, and reasoning. Examples must be relevant, diverse, and structured in <example>/<examples> tags. Including <thinking> tags in examples teaches Claude reasoning patterns. Anti-hallucination patterns include grounding in quotes and requiring file reading before answering.

key_concepts.definition: The recommended number of few-shot examples for best results. Fewer may not generalize; more yields diminishing returns.
XML tags that wrap few-shot examples to distinguish them from instructions. The outer <examples> groups all examples; each <example> contains one input/output pair.
Examples should be relevant (mirror actual use case), diverse (cover edge cases), and structured (clear input/output separation in tags).
Including reasoning steps in <thinking> tags within examples teaches Claude to generalize that reasoning style to its own extended thinking blocks.
Techniques to reduce hallucination: ground in quotes from source documents, require reading files before answering, use <investigate_before_answering> wrappers, include 'I don't know' examples.

title: Structured Output: JSON and Tool-Based
content:
Structured Output: JSON and Tool-Based

Claude provides two complementary mechanisms for generating structured data: JSON outputs via output_config.format and strict tool use via strict: true on tool definitions. Both use grammar-constrained sampling -- the model's token generation is constrained at sampling time to only produce tokens valid according to the schema. This is NOT post-processing validation; it is built into the generation process, guaranteeing 100% schema compliance.

output_config.format with json_schema

Constrain the entire response to a specific JSON schema:

{
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
  "max_tokens": 1024,
  "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Extract the key entities from this text: ..."}],
  "output_config": {
    "format": {
      "type": "json_schema",
      "schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "entities": {
            "type": "array",
            "items": {
              "type": "object",
              "properties": {
                "name": {"type": "string"},
                "type": {"type": "string", "enum": ["person", "org", "location"]}
              },
              "required": ["name", "type"],
              "additionalProperties": false
            }
          }
        },
        "required": ["entities"],
        "additionalProperties": false
      }
    }
  }
}

The response is valid JSON in response.content[0].text.

Schema Requirements

Critical Requirement

additionalProperties: false is required on every object in the schema. Omitting it causes a 400 error. This applies to both output_config.format and strict: true tool definitions.

FeatureSupported?Notes
enum, constYesScalars only for enum
anyOf, allOfYesallOf with $ref unsupported
$ref, $def, definitionsYesNo external $ref
String formatsYesdate-time, email, uri, uuid, etc.
pattern (regex)PartialNo backreferences, lookahead/lookbehind, \b
minItemsYesOnly 0 or 1
Recursive schemasNo
minimum/maximum/multipleOfNo
minLength/maxLengthNo

Complexity Limits

LimitValue
Strict tools per request20
Optional parameters (total)24
Union type parameters (anyOf/type arrays)16
Compilation timeout180 seconds

Exceeding combined grammar complexity returns a 400 error: "Schema is too complex for compilation."

Guarantees and Exceptions

Structured outputs provide:

  • Always valid JSON -- no JSON.parse() errors
  • Type safety -- guaranteed field types and required fields
  • No retries needed -- schema violations are impossible

Two exceptions where the schema may NOT be matched:

  1. Refusals (stop_reason: "refusal") -- safety takes precedence over schema compliance
  2. Token limit (stop_reason: "max_tokens") -- output is incomplete; retry with higher max_tokens

output_config.format vs tool_use Comparison

Featureoutput_config.formattool_use
Primary purposeConstrain entire response to JSONExtract structured data via function calling
Response locationcontent[0].text (JSON string)content[].input (on tool_use blocks)
Multiple schemasOne per requestMultiple tools per request
Mixing with textNo -- entire response is JSONYes -- text + tool_use blocks
Strict modeImplicit (always grammar-constrained)Opt-in via strict: true

SDK Helpers (Pydantic, Zod)

# Python  --  Pydantic model as output format
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Entities(BaseModel):
    entities: list[dict]

result = client.messages.parse(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Extract entities from..."}],
    output_format=Entities,
)
print(result.parsed_output)  # Typed Entities object
// TypeScript  --  Zod schema
import { zodOutputFormat } from "@anthropic-ai/sdk";
import { z } from "zod";

const schema = z.object({ entities: z.array(z.object({ name: z.string() })) });
const result = await client.messages.parse({
  model: "claude-sonnet-4-6",
  max_tokens: 1024,
  messages: [{ role: "user", content: "Extract entities..." }],
  ...zodOutputFormat(schema),
});
console.log(result.parsed_output); // Typed object

Grammar Compilation and 24h Cache

The first request with a new schema incurs additional latency for grammar compilation. Compiled grammars are automatically cached for 24 hours from last use. Cache is invalidated by schema structure changes or tool set changes; changing only name/description preserves the cache.

Pydantic Validation Patterns

Pydantic models serve as a single source of truth for structured output schemas. The Anthropic Python SDK can generate JSON Schemas directly from Pydantic models via client.messages.parse(), eliminating schema drift between your validation logic and the API contract.

Structural vs Semantic Validation

Validation TypeWhat It ChecksHow to Enforce
StructuralJSON shape, types, required fields, enum valuesGrammar-constrained sampling (strict: true) or output_config.format -- guaranteed by the API
SemanticBusiness logic: Is the date in the future? Does the total match line items? Is the entity real?Application-level Pydantic validators, post-hoc checks, or a separate validation API call

Structural validation is solved by grammar-constrained sampling -- it is impossible for the output to violate the schema. Semantic validation requires application logic because the API cannot enforce business rules.

Validate-Retry Loop Pattern

For semantic validation, implement a validate-retry loop:

  1. Call the API with structured output (Pydantic model or JSON schema)
  2. Parse the response into your Pydantic model (structural validation is guaranteed)
  3. Run Pydantic @field_validator and @model_validator checks for semantic correctness
  4. If semantic validation fails, re-prompt Claude with the specific validation errors
  5. Repeat up to a maximum retry count (typically 2-3 attempts)
from pydantic import BaseModel, field_validator

class Invoice(BaseModel):
    vendor: str
    total: float
    line_items: list[dict]

    @field_validator('total')
    @classmethod
    def total_must_match_lines(cls, v, info):
        items = info.data.get('line_items', [])
        expected = sum(item.get('amount', 0) for item in items)
        if abs(v - expected) > 0.01:
            raise ValueError(f'Total {v} does not match line items sum {expected}')
        return v

# Validate-retry loop
for attempt in range(3):
    result = client.messages.parse(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=messages,
        output_format=Invoice,
    )
    try:
        invoice = Invoice.model_validate(result.parsed_output.model_dump())
        break  # Semantic validation passed
    except ValidationError as e:
        messages.append({"role": "user", "content": f"Validation failed: {e}. Please fix."})

This pattern is especially valuable for document extraction where structural correctness (valid JSON) is guaranteed but semantic correctness (accurate data) requires domain-specific checks.

Exam-Critical Facts
  • additionalProperties: false is required on all objects
  • Max 20 strict tools, 24 optional params, 16 union types, 180s compilation timeout
  • Grammar compilation cache lasts 24 hours
  • Two exceptions: refusals and max_tokens truncation
  • Recursive schemas are not supported
  • output_config.format is always grammar-constrained; strict: true on tools is opt-in
  • Citations are incompatible with output_config.format (returns 400)
  • Pydantic models are single source of truth for JSON Schema generation -- no schema drift
  • Structural validation = API-guaranteed; Semantic validation = application-level Pydantic validators
  • Validate-retry loops: parse, validate semantics, re-prompt with errors (max 2-3 retries)
summary: Two mechanisms for structured output: output_config.format (constrains entire response to JSON schema) and strict tool use (strict: true on tool definitions). Both use grammar-constrained sampling for 100% schema compliance. Key requirements: additionalProperties: false on all objects, max 20 strict tools, 24 optional params, 180s compilation timeout, 24h grammar cache. Exceptions: refusals and max_tokens truncation.

key_concepts.definition: Token generation is constrained at sampling time to only produce valid tokens according to the schema. This is built into generation, not post-processing validation.
Required on every object in structured output schemas (both output_config.format and strict tool use). Omitting it causes a 400 error.
Max 20 strict tools, 24 optional parameters, 16 union type parameters per request. Exceeding combined complexity returns 400 error with 180-second compilation timeout.
output_config.format constrains the entire response to one JSON schema (always strict). tool_use allows multiple schemas per request and mixing text with structured data (strict is opt-in).
Compiled grammars are cached for 24 hours from last use. Schema structure changes invalidate the cache; name/description changes do not.
Two cases where output may not match schema: refusals (stop_reason: refusal, safety overrides schema) and max_tokens truncation (incomplete output, retry with higher limit).

title: Extended and Adaptive Thinking
content:
Extended and Adaptive Thinking

Extended thinking gives Claude the ability to reason internally before producing a response. Adaptive thinking is the current standard -- Claude dynamically decides when and how much to think based on the query complexity and a configurable effort level.

Adaptive Thinking Configuration

The recommended configuration uses thinking.type: "adaptive" combined with output_config.effort:

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-7",
    max_tokens=64000,
    thinking={"type": "adaptive"},
    output_config={"effort": "high"},
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Analyze this complex algorithm..."}],
)

Claude calibrates its internal reasoning based on two factors: the effort parameter and the actual complexity of the query. On easier queries that do not require deep reasoning, the model responds directly without extended thinking.

Five Effort Levels

LevelDescriptionUse Case
lowMinimal reasoning, fastestLatency-sensitive workloads, simple tasks
mediumGood cost-intelligence tradeoffCost-sensitive use cases, routine operations
highBalanced (equivalent to API default)Minimum for most intelligence-sensitive use cases
xhighExtended capability (Opus 4.7 only)Best for coding and agentic use cases
maxMaximum effort, may show diminishing returnsIntelligence-demanding tasks; can be prone to overthinking
Model-Specific Restriction

The xhigh effort level is only available on Claude Opus 4.7. Attempting to use it with other models will cause an error. For Opus 4.7, start with xhigh for coding and agentic tasks, and set max_tokens to at least 64k at xhigh/max effort.

high effort is equivalent to not setting the parameter -- it is the API default. Opus 4.7 respects effort levels strictly, especially at the low end. At low and medium, it scopes work to exactly what was asked.

Legacy budget_tokens (Deprecated on 4.6+, Error on 4.7)

The older manual thinking configuration used budget_tokens to set a token ceiling for reasoning:

{
  "thinking": {
    "type": "enabled",
    "budget_tokens": 10000,
    "display": "summarized"
  }
}
Deprecation Status
  • Claude Opus 4.7+: Returns 400 error -- manual thinking with budget_tokens is no longer supported
  • Claude Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6: Deprecated but still functional; will be removed in a future release
  • Other Claude models: Fully supported

Thinking Display

Display ModeBehaviorBilling
"summarized"Returns summarized thinking text (default on Opus/Sonnet 4.6)Charged for full thinking tokens, not summary
"omitted"Thinking blocks returned with empty text field (default on Opus 4.7/Mythos)Still charged for full thinking tokens

Both modes include a signature field containing encrypted full thinking for multi-turn continuity.

Interleaved Thinking

Claude thinks between tool calls and after receiving tool results. With adaptive thinking on Opus 4.6+, Sonnet 4.6, and Opus 4.7, interleaved thinking is automatic -- no special header required. For other Claude 4 models, the beta header interleaved-thinking-2025-05-14 is needed.

With interleaved thinking, budget_tokens can exceed max_tokens because it represents the total budget across all thinking blocks in the turn, not just one block.

Thinking in Multi-Turn: Pass Back Unmodified

When using extended thinking in multi-turn conversations with tools:

  • Include complete, unmodified thinking blocks in the last assistant message when continuing
  • The entire sequence of thinking blocks must match original outputs -- no rearranging or modifying
  • Thinking blocks from previous turns are stripped from context for cache calculation but still count as input tokens when read from cache
Cannot Toggle Mid-Turn

You cannot toggle thinking on/off during a tool use loop. The entire assistant turn must operate in a single thinking mode. Mid-turn conflicts trigger graceful degradation: thinking is silently disabled (no error thrown). Check for thinking blocks in the response to confirm whether thinking was active.

Prompt Caching with Thinking

  • Changes to thinking parameters invalidate message cache breakpoints
  • System prompts and tools remain cached despite thinking parameter changes
  • Cache TTL recommendation: "1h" for extended thinking (default is "5m")

Tool Use with Extended Thinking

When extended thinking is enabled, only tool_choice: {"type": "auto"} (default) or tool_choice: {"type": "none"} are supported. Using "any" or "tool" causes an error. Forcing specific tool use is incompatible with extended thinking.

Response Structure

{
  "content": [
    {
      "type": "thinking",
      "thinking": "Let me analyze this step by step...",
      "signature": "WaUjzkypQ2mUEVM36O2TxuC06KN8xyfbJwyem2dw3URve..."
    },
    {
      "type": "text",
      "text": "Based on my analysis..."
    }
  ]
}
Exam-Critical Facts
  • Adaptive thinking: thinking: {type: "adaptive"} + output_config: {effort: "..."}
  • Five effort levels: low, medium, high (default), xhigh (Opus 4.7 only), max
  • budget_tokens on Opus 4.7 = 400 error; on 4.6 = deprecated but functional
  • Display modes: "summarized" or "omitted" -- both charge for full thinking tokens
  • Extended thinking + tool_choice: only auto or none (any/tool = error)
  • Pass thinking blocks back unmodified for multi-turn continuity
  • Cannot toggle thinking mid-turn -- graceful degradation, not an error
summary: Adaptive thinking (thinking.type: 'adaptive' + output_config.effort) is the current standard for extended reasoning. Five effort levels from low to max, with xhigh exclusive to Opus 4.7. Legacy budget_tokens returns 400 on Opus 4.7, deprecated on 4.6. Thinking display modes (summarized/omitted) both bill for full tokens. Only tool_choice auto/none supported with thinking. Pass thinking blocks back unmodified in multi-turn.

key_concepts.definition: thinking.type: 'adaptive'  --  Claude dynamically decides when and how much to think based on effort level and query complexity. Recommended over manual budget_tokens.
low, medium, high (API default), xhigh (Opus 4.7 only, best for coding/agentic), max (maximum but may over-think). Effort controls thinking depth and output thoroughness.
Returns 400 error on Opus 4.7 (not supported). Deprecated but functional on Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6. Will be removed in future release.
summarized: returns summary text (default on 4.6). omitted: empty thinking field (default on 4.7). Both include signature for continuity. Both bill for full thinking tokens.
Claude thinks between tool calls and after receiving results. Automatic with adaptive thinking on 4.6+/4.7. Requires beta header on other Claude 4 models.
Extended thinking only supports tool_choice auto (default) or none. Using any or tool causes an error because forcing tool use is incompatible with extended reasoning.

title: Message Batches API
content:
Message Batches API

The Message Batches API enables asynchronous processing of large volumes of requests at 50% cost savings compared to standard API pricing. Batches process within a 24-hour window (most finish in under 1 hour) with no latency SLA.

Key Properties

PropertyValue
Cost savings50% off standard prices (input, output, and special tokens)
Processing windowUp to 24 hours; most batches complete in < 1 hour
Latency SLANone -- no guaranteed latency
Max requests per batch100,000 requests OR 256 MB, whichever comes first
Results availability29 days after creation
StreamingNot supported
Multi-turn tool callingNot supported (each request is independent)
Batch modificationCannot modify after submission; must cancel and resubmit

custom_id Requirements

Each request in a batch requires a custom_id for result correlation (results may return out of order).

  • Required for every request
  • Must be unique within the batch
  • 1-64 characters matching regex: ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{1,64}$
Common Misconception

The custom_id regex is identical to the tool name regex. Both use ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{1,64}$. This is a frequent exam question -- remember that alphanumeric characters, hyphens, and underscores are the only valid characters, and the max length is 64.

Processing States

StateDescription
in_progressInitial state; batch is being processed
cancelingCancel initiated but not yet complete
endedAll requests finished processing

Result Types

Result TypeDescriptionBilled?
succeededRequest completed successfullyYes
erroredRequest encountered an errorNo
canceledBatch canceled before this request processedNo
expiredBatch reached 24-hour expirationNo

Only succeeded results are billed. Failed, canceled, and expired requests cost nothing.

Error Handling

for result in client.messages.batches.results("msgbatch_..."):
    match result.result.type:
        case "succeeded":
            process(result.custom_id, result.result.message)
        case "errored":
            if result.result.error.error.type == "invalid_request_error":
                # Fix request body before retrying
                fix_and_resubmit(result.custom_id)
            else:
                # Server error  --  safe to retry directly
                retry_requests.append(result.custom_id)
        case "expired":
            # Resubmit; consider smaller batches
            retry_requests.append(result.custom_id)
        case "canceled":
            retry_requests.append(result.custom_id)

Key error handling rules:

  • invalid_request_error: Fix the request body before retrying (wrong schema, missing fields)
  • Server errors: Safe to retry directly without changes
  • Expired: Resubmit; consider breaking into smaller batches
  • Failure of one request does NOT affect processing of other requests

Prompt Caching with Batches

Prompt caching and batch discounts stack. Cache hits are best-effort due to async concurrent processing, with typical hit rates of 30%-98% depending on traffic patterns.

Tips for maximizing cache hits:

  • Include identical cache_control blocks across requests
  • Maintain a steady request stream
  • Use 1-hour cache duration instead of the default 5 minutes

Extended Output (300K tokens)

With the beta header output-300k-2026-03-24, batch requests can generate up to 300,000 output tokens:

  • Supported models: Claude Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6
  • Batch API only -- not available in synchronous Messages API
  • Not available on Bedrock, Vertex AI, or Microsoft Foundry
  • A single 300k-token generation can take over an hour

API Endpoints

OperationMethodPath
Create batchPOST/v1/messages/batches
Retrieve batchGET/v1/messages/batches/{batch_id}
List batchesGET/v1/messages/batches
Cancel batchPOST/v1/messages/batches/{batch_id}/cancel
Get resultsGETresults_url from batch object
Delete batchDELETE/v1/messages/batches/{batch_id}

Re-Submit Strategy for Failed Requests

The custom_id field is essential for the re-submit strategy. Since each request has a unique identifier, you can correlate results to requests and identify exactly which ones failed:

  1. Iterate results: Walk through all batch results, grouping by result type
  2. Identify failures by custom_id: Collect the custom_id values for errored, expired, and canceled results
  3. Classify errors: For errored results, check the error type -- invalid_request_error requires fixing the request body before resubmission; server errors can be retried directly
  4. Modify strategy if needed: For expired requests, consider smaller batch sizes; for invalid request errors, fix the request parameters
  5. Re-send only failed requests: Create a new batch containing only the failed requests (preserving or updating their custom_id values)
# Re-submit strategy
failed_requests = []
for result in client.messages.batches.results(batch_id):
    if result.result.type != "succeeded":
        original = original_requests_by_id[result.custom_id]
        if result.result.type == "errored" and result.result.error.error.type == "invalid_request_error":
            original = fix_request(original)  # Fix before resubmitting
        failed_requests.append(original)

if failed_requests:
    retry_batch = client.messages.batches.create(requests=failed_requests)

This pattern ensures you only pay for successful processing and never re-process already-succeeded requests.

Exam-Critical Facts
  • 50% cost savings, 24-hour window, no latency SLA
  • Max: 100,000 requests or 256 MB
  • custom_id: 1-64 chars, regex ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{1,64}$ (same as tool names)
  • Result types: succeeded, errored, canceled, expired -- only succeeded is billed
  • Results available for 29 days
  • Prompt caching hit rates: 30-98% with batches
  • Extended output: 300K tokens with beta header (batch only)
  • Cannot modify after submission -- cancel and resubmit
  • Re-submit strategy: identify failures by custom_id, fix invalid_request_error requests, re-send only the failed subset
Decision Guide: Batch API vs Synchronous
Is a developer/user waiting for the result?
Yes →
Use Synchronous API
No →
Can you wait up to 24 hours?
Yes →
Is it bulk processing (100+ requests)?
Yes →
Use Batch API (50% cost savings)
No →
Use Synchronous API
No →
Use Synchronous API
summary: Message Batches API provides 50% cost savings with a 24-hour processing window and no latency SLA. Max 100K requests or 256 MB per batch. Each request needs a unique custom_id (1-64 chars, same regex as tool names). Four result types: succeeded (billed), errored, canceled, expired. Prompt caching stacks with batch discounts (30-98% hit rates). Extended output supports 300K tokens via beta header.

key_concepts.definition: Batch API processes requests at half the standard API price for input, output, and special tokens. Trade-off is async processing with no latency guarantee.
Required unique identifier per request, 1-64 characters matching ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{1,64}$. Used to correlate results to requests since results may return out of order.
Four types: succeeded (billed), errored (not billed  --  fix or retry), canceled (not billed), expired (not billed  --  resubmit). Only succeeded results cost money.
Three states: in_progress (initial), canceling (cancel initiated), ended (all requests finished).
Beta header output-300k-2026-03-24 enables up to 300K output tokens per request. Batch API only, not available in sync Messages API. Supported on Opus 4.7, 4.6, Sonnet 4.6.
Cache discounts stack with batch discounts. Best-effort cache hits due to async processing, with 30-98% hit rates. Use 1-hour cache duration for best results.

title: Multi-Pass Review and Explicit Criteria
content:
Multi-Pass Review and Explicit Criteria

A single Claude instance reviewing its own output has inherent limitations -- the same biases that produced the original output may persist in self-review. The multi-pass pattern overcomes this by using independent API calls for generation, review, and refinement, giving each step fresh context and perspective.

Self-Review Limitations

Claude can self-check, and prompts like "Before you finish, verify your answer against [test criteria]" work well for coding and math tasks. However, this approach is less reliable for:

  • Subjective quality assessments
  • Bias detection in the model's own reasoning
  • Catching errors the model consistently makes

Self-review works within a single context window. The model that produced a flawed answer may have the same blind spots when reviewing it.

Independent Instance Pattern (Generate, Review, Refine)

The most common prompt chaining pattern uses separate API calls for each phase:

  1. Generate: Claude produces an initial draft or analysis
  2. Review: A separate API call reviews the draft against explicit criteria
  3. Refine: A third call incorporates review feedback into an improved version

Each step being a separate API call means independent context -- a fresh perspective. This also enables logging, evaluation, and branching at each step.

# Step 1: Generate
draft = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-7",
    max_tokens=4096,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Write a security review of:\n{code}"}]
)

# Step 2: Review (independent context)
review = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-7",
    max_tokens=4096,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Review this analysis for completeness:\n{draft.content[0].text}\n\nCheck: Are all OWASP Top 10 categories covered?"}]
)

# Step 3: Refine
final = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-7",
    max_tokens=4096,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Original analysis:\n{draft.content[0].text}\n\nReview feedback:\n{review.content[0].text}\n\nProduce an improved final version."}]
)

Finding Pass (Recall) vs Filtering Pass (Precision)

For tasks like code review or bug detection, split the work into two specialized passes:

PassObjectivePrompt Style
Finding Pass (Recall)Surface every possible issue"Report every issue you find, including ones you are uncertain about or consider low-severity. Do not filter for importance."
Filtering Pass (Precision)Deduplicate, rank, and filter"Rank these findings by severity and confidence. Remove duplicates and false positives."

The finding pass prompt is designed for maximum coverage:

Report every issue you find, including ones you are uncertain about
or consider low-severity. Do not filter for importance or confidence
at this stage  --  a separate verification step will do that. Your goal
here is coverage: it is better to surface a finding that later gets
filtered out than to silently drop a real bug. For each finding,
include your confidence level and an estimated severity so a
downstream filter can rank them.

Opus 4.7 Literal Interpretation

Claude Opus 4.7 interprets prompts more literally and explicitly than Opus 4.6, especially at lower effort levels. Key behavioral differences:

  • Will NOT silently generalize an instruction from one item to another
  • Will NOT infer requests you did not make
  • May follow "only report high-severity issues" so faithfully that it thoroughly investigates bugs but then does not report findings it judges below your stated bar
Practical Impact

If you say "be conservative" to Opus 4.7, precision rises but recall falls. Be concrete about the bar: "Report any bugs that could cause incorrect behavior, a test failure, or a misleading result; only omit nits like pure style or naming preferences."

If you need an instruction applied broadly, state the scope explicitly: "Apply this formatting to every section, not just the first one."

Per-File + Cross-File Passes

For code review harnesses operating on large changesets:

  • Per-file pass: Analyze each file independently for issues, collecting findings with confidence and severity
  • Cross-file pass: Analyze relationships between files -- data flow, interface contracts, consistency

Claude maintains orientation across extended sessions. Use structured state files (e.g., a running JSON document of findings) to track progress across files.

Confidence and Severity

Each finding should include two dimensions for downstream filtering:

  • Confidence: How certain is the reviewer that this is a real issue? (high/medium/low)
  • Severity: What is the potential impact? (critical/high/medium/low)

These fields enable the filtering pass to make informed triage decisions rather than relying on qualitative terms.

For code review: Claude Opus 4.7

Opus 4.7 shows 11 percentage points better recall on bug-finding evaluations compared to prior models. This makes it the preferred choice for the finding pass, where recall is the primary metric.

Exam-Critical Facts
  • Self-review has limitations -- same biases persist in single-instance review
  • Use separate API calls for Generate → Review → Refine (independent context)
  • Finding pass = high recall; Filtering pass = high precision
  • Opus 4.7 interprets prompts more literally -- be explicit about scope and thresholds
  • "Be conservative" on Opus 4.7 may suppress valid findings -- use concrete criteria instead
  • Per-file + cross-file passes for large codebases
  • Always include confidence and severity in findings for downstream filtering
summary: Multi-pass review uses independent API calls for generation, review, and refinement to overcome single-instance self-review limitations. Split tasks into a finding pass (maximize recall) and filtering pass (maximize precision). Opus 4.7 interprets instructions more literally  --  be explicit about scope and concrete about severity thresholds. Always include confidence and severity in findings.

key_concepts.definition: A single Claude instance reviewing its own output shares the same biases that produced it. Works for coding/math verification but is less reliable for subjective quality or bias detection.
Three separate API calls: generate a draft, review against explicit criteria, refine incorporating feedback. Each call has independent context for fresh perspective.
Finding pass prioritizes recall (surface every issue, even uncertain ones). Filtering pass prioritizes precision (deduplicate, rank by severity, remove false positives).
Opus 4.7 follows instructions more literally than 4.6, especially at lower effort. Will not generalize across items or infer unstated requests. State scope and criteria explicitly.
Two dimensions that each finding should include for downstream filtering. Confidence = certainty it is a real issue. Severity = potential impact. Enables informed triage.

title: Messages API Reference
content:
Messages API Reference

The Messages API (POST /v1/messages) is the primary interface for all Claude interactions. Understanding its parameters, defaults, and response structure is essential for building correct integrations.

Required Parameters

ParameterTypeDescription
modelstringModel ID (e.g., claude-opus-4-7, claude-sonnet-4-6)
messagesarrayArray of message objects (alternating user/assistant roles)
max_tokensnumberMaximum tokens to generate (absolute ceiling, not target)

All three are required -- omitting any one causes a 400 error.

Key Optional Parameters

ParameterTypeDefaultDescription
systemstring or TextBlockParam[] -- System prompt (supports cache_control on text blocks)
temperaturenumber (0.0-1.0)1.0Randomness control; 0.0 for analytical, 1.0 for creative
toolsToolUnion[] -- Tool definitions (client and server)
tool_choiceobject{"type": "auto"}How model selects tools (auto/any/tool/none)
thinkingobject -- Extended thinking config: adaptive, enabled, or disabled
output_configobject -- Output format (json_schema) and effort level
streambooleanfalseEnable SSE streaming
stop_sequencesstring[] -- Custom stop sequences
metadataobject -- Contains user_id for abuse detection (UUID/hash, never PII)
service_tierstring"auto""auto" or "standard_only"
Temperature Default: 1.0 (NOT 0.7)

A common misconception from other LLM APIs: Claude's default temperature is 1.0, not 0.7. The range is 0.0-1.0. Even at 0.0, outputs are not fully deterministic. For analytical/structured tasks, set temperature to 0.0; for creative tasks, leave at default.

output_config Full Syntax

{
  "output_config": {
    "format": {
      "type": "json_schema",
      "schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": { ... },
        "required": [...],
        "additionalProperties": false
      }
    },
    "effort": "low" | "medium" | "high" | "xhigh" | "max"
  }
}

Both format and effort can be set independently or together within output_config.

Content Block Types

User-side blocks:

  • text -- text content
  • image -- base64 or URL image
  • document -- PDF or text document
  • tool_result -- tool execution result

Assistant-side blocks:

  • text -- generated text
  • tool_use -- client tool call request (id prefix: toolu_)
  • server_tool_use -- server tool call (id prefix: srvtoolu_)
  • thinking -- extended thinking block (with signature field)
  • redacted_thinking -- thinking that was redacted for safety

Response Fields

{
  "id": "msg_01XYZ...",
  "type": "message",
  "role": "assistant",
  "content": [
    {"type": "text", "text": "Here is the analysis..."}
  ],
  "model": "claude-opus-4-7",
  "stop_reason": "end_turn",
  "stop_sequence": null,
  "usage": {
    "input_tokens": 245,
    "output_tokens": 312,
    "cache_creation_input_tokens": 1200,
    "cache_read_input_tokens": 0
  }
}

stop_reason values: end_turn, stop_sequence, max_tokens, tool_use, refusal

Cache Control

Prompt caching reduces costs by caching and reusing repeated content prefixes. Applied via cache_control blocks:

{
  "system": [
    {
      "type": "text",
      "text": "You are a helpful assistant with extensive domain knowledge.",
      "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}
    }
  ]
}

Cache TTL options:

  • "5m" -- 5 minutes (default, implicit when only {"type": "ephemeral"} is set)
  • "1h" -- 1 hour (recommended for extended thinking and batch processing)

Cache tokens are tracked in the response: cache_creation_input_tokens (first write) and cache_read_input_tokens (subsequent hits).

Service Tier

ValueBehavior
"auto" (default)Routes to higher capacity when available; may include additional processing tiers
"standard_only"Forces standard processing tier only; use when you need consistent latency characteristics

Complete Request Example

{
  "model": "claude-opus-4-7",
  "max_tokens": 4096,
  "system": "You are a code review assistant.",
  "temperature": 0.0,
  "thinking": {"type": "adaptive"},
  "output_config": {
    "effort": "high",
    "format": {
      "type": "json_schema",
      "schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "issues": {
            "type": "array",
            "items": {
              "type": "object",
              "properties": {
                "file": {"type": "string"},
                "line": {"type": "integer"},
                "severity": {"type": "string", "enum": ["critical", "high", "medium", "low"]},
                "description": {"type": "string"}
              },
              "required": ["file", "line", "severity", "description"],
              "additionalProperties": false
            }
          }
        },
        "required": ["issues"],
        "additionalProperties": false
      }
    }
  },
  "messages": [
    {"role": "user", "content": "Review this code for bugs:\n```python\ndef divide(a, b):\n    return a / b\n```"}
  ]
}
Exam-Critical Facts
  • Three required parameters: model, messages, max_tokens
  • Temperature default is 1.0 (NOT 0.7) -- range 0.0-1.0
  • stop_reason values: end_turn, stop_sequence, max_tokens, tool_use, refusal
  • Cache control TTL: "5m" (default) or "1h" (recommended for thinking/batches)
  • service_tier: "auto" (default) or "standard_only"
  • output_config holds both format (JSON schema) and effort (thinking depth)
  • Client tool blocks use toolu_ prefix; server tools use srvtoolu_
  • metadata.user_id should be UUID/hash, never PII
summary: The Messages API (POST /v1/messages) requires model, messages, and max_tokens. Temperature defaults to 1.0 (not 0.7). Key optional params include system, tools, thinking, output_config (format + effort), and cache_control (5m default, 1h recommended for thinking). Response includes stop_reason (end_turn, tool_use, max_tokens, stop_sequence, refusal) and usage with cache token tracking.

key_concepts.definition: Three required: model (string model ID), messages (alternating user/assistant array), max_tokens (absolute ceiling, not a target). Omitting any causes 400 error.
Claude's default temperature is 1.0, not 0.7 like some other LLMs. Range is 0.0-1.0. Even at 0.0, outputs are not fully deterministic.
Five values: end_turn (finished), tool_use (wants to call tools), max_tokens (hit limit), stop_sequence (matched custom sequence), refusal (safety decline).
Applied via cache_control: {type: 'ephemeral'} with TTL of '5m' (default) or '1h'. Tracks cache_creation_input_tokens and cache_read_input_tokens in usage.
Contains both format (json_schema for structured output) and effort (low/medium/high/xhigh/max for thinking depth). Both can be set independently or together.
User-side: text, image, document, tool_result. Assistant-side: text, tool_use (toolu_ prefix), server_tool_use (srvtoolu_ prefix), thinking, redacted_thinking.
auto (default, routes to higher capacity when available) or standard_only (consistent latency). Controls request routing, not model selection.

title: Context Windows and Context Rot
content:
Model Context Windows

The context window is the total text a model can reference when generating a response, including the response itself. It serves as the model's "working memory," distinct from training data. Understanding context window sizes is foundational to context management because they define the hard limits within which all strategies must operate.

ModelContext WindowNotes
Claude Mythos Preview1M tokensLatest preview model
Claude Opus 4.71M tokensMost capable GA model
Claude Opus 4.61M tokens
Claude Sonnet 4.61M tokens
Claude Sonnet 4.5200K tokensDeprecated predecessor
Other Claude models200K tokensDefault for non-1M models

Context Window Structure

The context window consists of two phases that together must fit within the model's token limit:

  • Input phase: System prompt + tools configuration + images + conversation history + current user message
  • Output phase: The generated text response, which becomes part of future input in multi-turn conversations

Growth is linear: each turn accumulates in the window because previous turns are preserved completely. This linear accumulation is precisely why context management strategies are necessary for long-running agent sessions.

Context Rot: The Official Anthropic Term

As token count grows, accuracy and recall degrade. Anthropic officially calls this phenomenon context rot. This is the canonical term used in Anthropic's documentation.

Exam Precision: Context Rot vs. Lost in the Middle

"Context rot" is the official Anthropic term for the degradation of accuracy and recall as token count grows. "Lost in the middle" is a related but distinct concept from academic research about position-based retrieval difficulty. On the exam, use "context rot" when referring to Anthropic's documented phenomenon.

Why Context Rot Happens: N-Squared Degradation

The Transformer architecture creates n-squared pairwise token relationships through its attention mechanism. As context grows, models struggle to capture these relationships effectively. Position encoding interpolation allows longer sequences but introduces degradation. LLMs possess a limited "attention budget" comparable to human working memory, and each token depletes this budget.

The critical insight from Anthropic's documentation: more context is NOT automatically better. Curating what is in context is just as important as how much space is available. Claude achieves state-of-the-art results on long-context benchmarks (MRCR, GraphWalks), but gains depend on what is in context, not just how much fits.

Position Effects

For inputs exceeding 20K tokens, the position of information within the context window matters significantly. Official prompting best practices prescribe specific positioning:

  1. Put longform data at the top: Place long documents and inputs near the TOP of the prompt, above queries, instructions, and examples
  2. Put queries at the end: Placing the query at the end can improve response quality by up to 30% in tests, especially with complex, multi-document inputs
  3. Structure with XML tags: When using multiple documents, wrap each in <document> tags with metadata subtags
  4. Ground responses in quotes: Ask Claude to quote relevant parts FIRST before carrying out its task to "cut through the noise"
<documents>
  <document index="1">
    <source>annual_report_2023.pdf</source>
    <document_content>
      {{ANNUAL_REPORT}}
    </document_content>
  </document>
</documents>

<!-- Query goes AFTER documents for up to 30% improvement -->
Analyze the annual report. Identify strategic advantages.

Context Awareness Tags

Select models (Claude Sonnet 4.6, Sonnet 4.5, Haiku 4.5) feature context awareness — they track remaining token budget throughout a conversation. At conversation start, Claude receives:

<budget:token_budget>1000000</budget:token_budget>

After each tool call, Claude receives an update:

<system_warning>Token usage: 35000/1000000; 965000 remaining</system_warning>

Anthropic's analogy: without context awareness, an agent is like competing in a cooking show without a clock. This feature is critical for long-running agent sessions requiring sustained focus and for multi-context-window workflows where state transitions matter.

Image and PDF Limits

Model TierMax Images/PDF Pages
1M-token modelsUp to 600 per request
200K-token modelsUp to 100 per request

Note: You may hit request size limits before token limits when sending many images or documents.

Key Numbers for the Exam
  • 1M context: Mythos Preview, Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6
  • 200K context: Sonnet 4.5, Sonnet 4, and other older models
  • Position effect: up to 30% improvement with query at end
  • Image/PDF limits: 600 (1M models) vs 100 (200K models)
  • Context rot is the official Anthropic term, driven by n-squared attention degradation
summary: The context window is a model's working memory: input (system prompt, tools, conversation history) plus output. 1M-token models include Mythos Preview, Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, and Sonnet 4.6; all others are 200K. Context rot is Anthropic's official term for accuracy/recall degradation as token count grows, caused by n-squared pairwise attention. Position effects can yield up to 30% improvement by placing queries at the end of long documents. Context awareness tags give select models a running token budget counter.

key_concepts.definition: Anthropic's official term for the degradation of accuracy and recall as token count grows in the context window, driven by n-squared pairwise attention relationships.
Composed of input phase (system prompt + tools + images + conversation history + user message) and output phase (generated response). Growth is linear across turns.
For 20K+ token inputs, placing longform data at top and queries at end improves response quality by up to 30%.
Feature of Sonnet 4.6, Sonnet 4.5, and Haiku 4.5 that tracks remaining token budget via XML tags, enabling agents to manage long sessions without premature stopping.
1M-token models support up to 600 images/PDF pages per request; 200K-token models support up to 100.
The Transformer attention mechanism creates pairwise relationships between all tokens, making attention budget finite and context curation essential.

title: Context Management Strategies
content:
Why Context Management Matters

Context rot makes unmanaged context windows progressively less useful. The solution is not simply having a larger window — it is curating what occupies that window. Anthropic provides five explicit strategies for managing context, plus server-side compaction and context editing APIs for automated management.

Strategy 1: Trim Verbose Tool Outputs

Removing raw tool outputs from deep message history is described in Anthropic's context engineering guide as "one of the safest, lightest touch forms of compaction." Tool results from early in a conversation (e.g., large file reads, search results) rapidly consume tokens without providing ongoing value.

The API supports this via context editing's clear_tool_uses_20250919 strategy, which clears oldest tool results in chronological order and replaces them with placeholders.

Strategy 2: Structured Fact Extraction with XML

Rather than dumping raw documents into context, extract and structure facts using XML tags. This reduces token consumption while preserving information density:

<documents>
  <document index="1">
    <source>quarterly_report.pdf</source>
    <document_content>
      {{EXTRACTED_FACTS}}
    </document_content>
  </document>
</documents>

Nest tags when content has a natural hierarchy. The official guidance emphasizes using <document>, <document_content>, and <source> subtags for structured document ingestion.

Strategy 3: Just-In-Time Retrieval with Lightweight Identifiers

Instead of loading everything upfront, maintain lightweight identifiers (file paths, URLs, database keys) and dynamically load data at runtime using tools. This is the JIT approach:

  • Keep references small — file paths and links, not full contents
  • Load data on demand when the agent actually needs it
  • Use hybrid strategies: retrieve some data upfront for speed while enabling autonomous exploration

Strategy 4: System Prompt Optimization

System prompts require "the minimal set of information that fully outlines expected behavior." Every token in the system prompt is repeated on every API call, making bloated system prompts especially costly:

  • Tools should be self-contained, unambiguous, and minimal in overlap
  • Examples should portray canonical behavior patterns rather than exhaustive edge cases
  • Avoid duplicating information between system prompt and tool descriptions

Strategy 5: Structured Note-Taking (External Files)

Agents maintain persistent external memory via files (progress.txt, todo lists) and pull information back into context as needed. This enables long-horizon coherence across thousands of steps by offloading state to the filesystem rather than relying on context window persistence.

Server-Side Compaction

Server-side compaction is Anthropic's recommended strategy for managing long conversations. It automatically summarizes older context when approaching the context window limit.

Beta Header

Beta header required: anthropic-beta: compact-2026-01-12. Strategy type: compact_20260112. ZDR eligible.

Supported Models

Claude Mythos Preview, Claude Opus 4.7, Claude Opus 4.6, Claude Sonnet 4.6.

Configuration

{
  "context_management": {
    "edits": [
      {
        "type": "compact_20260112",
        "trigger": {
          "type": "input_tokens",
          "value": 150000
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}
ParameterDefaultDescription
typeRequiredMust be "compact_20260112"
trigger150,000 tokensWhen to trigger (minimum 50,000 tokens)
pause_after_compactionfalsePause after generating summary; returns stop_reason: "compaction"
instructionsnullCustom summarization prompt (replaces default entirely, does not supplement)

How Compaction Works

  1. Detects when input tokens exceed configured trigger threshold
  2. Generates a summary of the current conversation
  3. Creates a compaction block containing the summary
  4. Continues the response with compacted context
  5. On subsequent requests, API automatically drops all message blocks prior to the compaction block

Pause After Compaction

When pause_after_compaction: true, API returns with stop_reason: "compaction" after generating the summary. This allows adding additional content blocks before continuing. Use case: enforcing total token budget across long agentic loops by counting compactions.

Compaction Token Tracking

Top-level input_tokens and output_tokens do NOT include compaction costs. You must sum across all usage.iterations to get the true billed amount. For example: compaction (180K input + 3.5K output) + message (23K input + 1K output) = 207.5K total billed tokens.

Context Editing (Beta)

Context editing selectively clears specific content from conversation history as it grows. It is server-side (applied before prompt reaches Claude) while your client maintains the full unmodified history.

Beta header required: anthropic-beta: context-management-2025-06-27. ZDR eligible. Available on all supported Claude models.

Two server-side strategies are available:

StrategyWhat It ClearsKey Config
clear_tool_uses_20250919Oldest tool results chronologically, replaced with placeholderkeep: 3 (recent pairs), trigger at 100K tokens
clear_thinking_20251015Thinking blocks from prior turnskeep: {type: "thinking_turns", value: 1} (default)

Tool Result Clearing Configuration (clear_tool_uses_20250919)

OptionDefaultDescription
trigger100,000 input tokensWhen clearing activates (input_tokens or tool_uses)
keep3 tool usesHow many recent tool use/result pairs to keep
clear_at_leastNoneMinimum tokens to clear each time (prevents wasteful cache invalidation)
exclude_toolsNoneTool names to never clear
clear_tool_inputsfalseWhether to also clear tool call parameters (not just results)

Thinking Block Clearing Configuration (clear_thinking_20251015)

Default behavior (without config): keeps only thinking blocks from last assistant turn, equivalent to keep: {type: "thinking_turns", value: 1}.

The keep parameter accepts:

  • {type: "thinking_turns", value: N} where N > 0 — keep thinking from last N turns
  • "all" — keep all thinking blocks (maximizes cache hits)

Critical ordering rule: When combining strategies, clear_thinking_20251015 must be listed FIRST in the edits array.

Prompt Caching Interaction

  • Tool result clearing: Invalidates cached prompt prefixes when content is cleared
  • Thinking block clearing: Keeping blocks preserves cache; clearing invalidates cache at the clearing point

Response Format

context_management.applied_edits in the response shows which edits were applied, including cleared_tool_uses, cleared_thinking_turns, and cleared_input_tokens.

Token Counting Endpoint

The /v1/messages/count_tokens endpoint supports context management. It reports input_tokens (after clearing) and context_management.original_input_tokens (before clearing), allowing you to see the effect of context editing before sending the actual request.

The /compact Command in Claude Code

In Claude Code, /compact triggers context compaction within a session. What survives:

  • Project-root CLAUDE.md: Re-read from disk and re-injected after /compact
  • Invoked skills: Re-attached (first 5,000 tokens each, combined budget 25,000 tokens, most recently invoked first)
  • Nested CLAUDE.md files: NOT re-injected automatically
  • Conversation-only instructions: LOST unless saved to CLAUDE.md

When to Start Fresh vs. Compact

Start Fresh WhenUse Compaction When
State is well-persisted in files (progress.txt, tests.json, git)Conversation context contains unpersisted information
Compaction has accumulated too many layers of summarizationMid-task continuity is critical
Claude can rediscover context from the filesystemStarting fresh would lose important conversational context

Coverage Annotations

When Claude processes documents but cannot fully cover all content (due to length, complexity, or time constraints), it should annotate its output with coverage metadata:

  • PARTIAL COVERAGE tag: Clearly mark when only a subset of the source material was processed, with a note explaining why (e.g., "timeout after processing 15 of 30 pages")
  • Provenance tracking fields: Each extracted fact or claim should carry metadata including source_url, publication_date, and confidence level
{
  "coverage": "PARTIAL COVERAGE: processed pages 1-15 of 30 (timeout)",
  "facts": [
    {
      "claim": "Revenue grew 12% YoY",
      "source_url": "annual_report_2025.pdf",
      "publication_date": "2025-03-15",
      "confidence": "high"
    }
  ]
}

This pattern ensures downstream consumers know the completeness and provenance of extracted information, enabling informed decisions about whether to accept or re-process.

Key Takeaways
  • Five strategies: trim tool outputs, structured extraction, JIT retrieval, system prompt optimization, structured note-taking
  • Compaction beta: compact-2026-01-12; type: compact_20260112; trigger minimum: 50K tokens; default: 150K
  • Context editing beta: context-management-2025-06-27; ZDR eligible
  • Tool result clearing options: keep, clear_at_least, exclude_tools, clear_tool_inputs
  • Thinking block clearing: keep accepts {type: "thinking_turns", value: N} or "all"
  • Context editing ordering: clear_thinking must be listed FIRST in edits array
  • Token counting: /v1/messages/count_tokens shows before/after clearing token counts
  • pause_after_compaction: true returns stop_reason: "compaction"
  • Skills budget after compaction: 25,000 tokens total, 5,000 per skill
  • Prefer fresh start when state is well-persisted externally; use compaction when conversational context is critical
  • Use PARTIAL COVERAGE annotations when processing is incomplete
  • Track provenance with source_url, publication_date, confidence per fact
Common Anti-Patterns
  • Anti-pattern: Summarizing numbers, percentages, and dates -- these precise values are lost in compression and cannot be recovered
    Instead: Extract precise values into a structured <extracted_facts> block that persists outside the summarized history; summaries should reference these facts, not contain them
  • Anti-pattern: Not using an extracted facts block persistent outside summarized history
    Instead: Maintain a structured JSON or XML block of key facts (numbers, dates, names) that survives compaction separately from narrative summaries
  • Anti-pattern: Not delegating to subagents to protect the coordinator's context window
    Instead: Use subagents for side tasks that would flood the main context with verbose tool outputs; subagents process data in isolation and return only a condensed summary (~93% context savings)
  • Anti-pattern: Using sentiment analysis or model confidence as an escalation trigger
    Instead: Use explicit, deterministic criteria for escalation: checkpoints, blockers, and max iteration counts -- never rely on the model's self-assessed confidence or tone analysis
summary: Five core strategies for managing context: trimming verbose tool outputs (safest lightweight compaction), structured fact extraction with XML, JIT retrieval with lightweight identifiers, system prompt optimization, and structured note-taking in external files. Server-side compaction (compact_20260112, beta: compact-2026-01-12) auto-summarizes at configurable thresholds (min 50K, default 150K tokens); pause_after_compaction returns stop_reason: compaction. Context editing (beta: context-management-2025-06-27) offers selective clearing of tool results (keep, clear_at_least, exclude_tools, clear_tool_inputs) and thinking blocks (keep with thinking_turns or all). Token counting endpoint /v1/messages/count_tokens shows pre/post-clearing token counts. In Claude Code, /compact re-injects CLAUDE.md and recently invoked skills (25K budget). Choose fresh start when state is persisted externally; compact when conversational context matters.

key_concepts.definition: API feature (compact_20260112) that automatically summarizes conversation history when input tokens exceed a configurable trigger threshold (min 50K, default 150K).

Beta API feature (beta: context-management-2025-06-27) for selectively clearing tool results (clear_tool_uses_20250919) and thinking blocks (clear_thinking_20251015) from conversation history. Server-side, ZDR eligible.

Five options for clear_tool_uses_20250919: keep (recent pairs to keep, default 3), trigger (when to activate, default 100K tokens), clear_at_least (minimum tokens per clearing), exclude_tools (tool names to never clear), clear_tool_inputs (also clear call parameters, default false).
clear_thinking_20251015 keep parameter accepts {type: thinking_turns, value: N} to keep last N turns, or 'all' to keep all thinking blocks (maximizes cache hits). Default keeps last 1 turn.
/v1/messages/count_tokens supports context management, showing input_tokens (after clearing) and context_management.original_input_tokens (before clearing).
Maintaining lightweight identifiers (file paths, links) in context and dynamically loading full data at runtime using tools, rather than loading everything upfront.
Top-level usage fields exclude compaction costs. Must sum all usage.iterations to compute true billed tokens.
In Claude Code, re-reads project CLAUDE.md from disk and re-attaches recently invoked skills (5K each, 25K total). Conversation-only instructions are lost.
Start fresh when state is well-persisted in files and filesystem; use compaction when mid-task continuity or conversational context is critical.

title: State Persistence and Crash Recovery
content:
Why State Persistence Matters

Compaction alone "isn't sufficient" for maintaining agent coherence across long tasks. Anthropic's harnesses guide documents that without structural guidance, agents fail across context windows by attempting too much simultaneously and prematurely declaring projects complete. State persistence patterns solve this by externalizing critical information to the filesystem where it survives compaction, crashes, and session boundaries.

The progress.txt Pattern (Unstructured Notes)

The progress.txt file serves as an unstructured scratchpad for general progress notes, context, and next steps. It is deliberately freeform to capture nuances that structured formats cannot:

// Progress notes (progress.txt)
Session 3 progress:
- Fixed authentication token validation
- Updated user model to handle edge cases
- Next: investigate user_management test failures (test #2)
- Note: Do not remove tests as this could lead to missing functionality

The agent reads this file at the start of each session or after compaction to recover context about where it left off and what decisions were made.

The tests.json Pattern (Structured State)

For data that must be machine-readable and precise, structured JSON files track test status, feature completion, and other schema-bound data:

{
  "tests": [
    { "id": 1, "name": "authentication_flow", "status": "passing" },
    { "id": 2, "name": "user_management", "status": "failing" },
    { "id": 3, "name": "api_endpoints", "status": "not_started" }
  ],
  "total": 200,
  "passing": 150,
  "failing": 25,
  "not_started": 25
}
Critical Rule from Official Docs

Agents should edit this file only by changing the status of a passes field. "It is unacceptable to remove or edit tests." This constraint prevents agents from "solving" test failures by deleting the tests.

State Management Best Practices

TypeFormatUse For
Structured stateJSON (tests.json, feature_list.json)Test results, task status, schema-bound data
Unstructured notesText (progress.txt)General progress, context, next steps
Version controlGit commitsCheckpoints, rollback, history of changes
Setup scriptsShell (init.sh)Eliminating repeated server startup overhead

Git as a Recovery Mechanism

Git is a first-class recovery mechanism for agent systems. Agents are instructed to commit after each feature with descriptive messages, creating checkpoints that enable:

  • Rollback of failed implementations
  • A log of what has been accomplished
  • Checkpoints that can be restored after crashes
  • Cross-session state tracking (Claude's latest models "perform especially well in using git to track state across multiple sessions")

Multi-Context Window Workflow

The official pattern for complex projects that exceed a single context window:

  1. First context window: Set up framework (write tests, create setup scripts)
  2. Subsequent windows: Iterate on a todo-list
  3. Write tests in structured format: Create tests before starting work, track in structured format (tests.json)
  4. Set up quality of life tools: Create init.sh to start servers, run test suites, linters
  5. Provide verification tools: Playwright MCP, computer use for testing UIs
  6. Encourage complete usage: "Continue working systematically until you have completed this task"

Two-Agent Architecture

From Anthropic's harnesses guide, a production-proven pattern:

  1. Initializer Agent: First session only. Sets up the foundation — writes tests, creates scripts, establishes the framework
  2. Coding Agent: All subsequent sessions. Makes incremental progress on the task list established by the initializer

This separation ensures the setup work (which is done once) does not consume context in iterative coding sessions.

Session Startup Sequence (Recovery Pattern)

When recovering from a crash or starting a new session, the official pattern prescribes:

  1. Run pwd to confirm working directory
  2. Read git logs and progress files
  3. Review feature list and select highest-priority incomplete feature
  4. Start development server
  5. Execute basic end-to-end verification test before new work

This approach "saves Claude some tokens in every session since it doesn't have to figure out how to test."

Context Awareness Prompt Pattern

The official prompt pattern for context-aware agents instructs them to persist through context boundaries:

Your context window will be automatically compacted as it approaches its
limit, allowing you to continue working indefinitely from where you left
off. Therefore, do not stop tasks early due to token budget concerns. As
you approach your token budget limit, save your current progress and state
to memory before the context window refreshes. Always be as persistent and
autonomous as possible and complete tasks fully, even if the end of your
budget is approaching. Never artificially stop any task early regardless
of the context remaining.
Key Takeaways
  • Compaction alone is insufficient — external state files are required for reliable long-running agents
  • Use progress.txt for freeform notes and tests.json for structured, machine-readable state
  • Git commits after each feature serve as crash recovery checkpoints
  • Two-Agent Architecture: Initializer sets up, Coding Agent iterates
  • Session startup: pwd, read logs, review features, start server, verify tests
  • Never remove tests — only change status fields
summary: State persistence externalizes critical information to the filesystem to survive compaction, crashes, and session boundaries. Two complementary patterns: progress.txt for unstructured notes and tests.json for structured state. Git commits after each feature serve as recovery checkpoints. The Two-Agent Architecture separates initialization from iterative coding. The official session startup sequence reads git logs and progress files, reviews features, and runs verification tests before new work. The context awareness prompt pattern instructs agents to persist through context boundaries without prematurely stopping.

key_concepts.definition: Unstructured text file for recording general progress, decisions, and next steps. Read at session start to recover context.
Structured JSON file tracking test status. Agents may only change status fields, never remove or edit test definitions.
Commit after each feature with descriptive messages, creating checkpoints for rollback and cross-session state tracking.
Initializer Agent sets up foundation (tests, scripts) in first session; Coding Agent makes incremental progress in subsequent sessions.
First window sets up framework and tests; subsequent windows iterate on a structured todo-list with verification tools.
pwd -> read git logs/progress files -> review features -> start server -> verify tests before new work.

title: Escalation and Human Review Patterns
content:
Escalation in Agent Systems

Escalation patterns define when an autonomous agent should pause, seek human input, or hand off to a specialized handler. Effective escalation balances agent autonomy against the risk of compounding errors. The key principle from Anthropic: escalation should be based on explicit criteria, not sentiment analysis or guesswork.

Escalation Criteria

From Anthropic's Building Effective Agents guide, three primary triggers for escalation:

TriggerDescriptionExample
CheckpointsPredetermined points where human review is requiredAfter generating a deployment plan, before executing it
BlockersAgent encounters something it cannot resolve autonomouslyMissing credentials, ambiguous requirements, conflicting specifications
Max iterationsSafety stopping condition to prevent runaway loopsAgent has attempted 10 retries on a failing test without progress

Agents must obtain "ground truth from the environment at each step" via tool results and code execution feedback. When ground truth is unavailable or contradictory, escalation is warranted.

Routing for Specialized Handlers

The routing pattern from Anthropic's workflow patterns classifies inputs and directs them to specialized downstream handlers. This enables separation of concerns and task-specific prompt optimization:

  • Classify the incoming task by type (technical, billing, security, etc.)
  • Route to a handler with specialized prompts, tools, and context
  • Each handler can have its own escalation thresholds

The Evaluator-Optimizer pattern extends this: one LLM generates output, another provides iterative feedback. This is effective when clear evaluation criteria exist.

Autonomy Balance: Reversible vs. Irreversible Actions

Anthropic's official guidance for Claude Code draws a clear line between actions that should proceed autonomously and those requiring confirmation:

Consider the reversibility and potential impact of your actions. You are
encouraged to take local, reversible actions like editing files or running
tests, but for actions that are hard to reverse, affect shared systems, or
could be destructive, ask the user before proceeding.

Examples that warrant confirmation:
- Destructive operations: deleting files or branches, dropping database tables
- Hard to reverse: git push --force, git reset --hard
- Operations visible to others: pushing code, commenting on PRs/issues
The Reversibility Heuristic

Reversible actions = proceed locally. Editing files, running tests, reading data. Irreversible actions = confirm first. Deleting resources, force pushing, modifying shared infrastructure. This heuristic is central to Anthropic's agent safety philosophy.

Field-Level Confidence

When Claude extracts structured data, it should assign confidence at the field level rather than the document level:

ConfidenceCriteriaAction
HighDirectly stated, unambiguousAccept with standard QA sampling
MediumInferred from context, partially statedFlag for targeted review
LowSpeculative, conflicting sourcesRequire human verification

Stratified Random Sampling for QA

For quality assurance of AI outputs at scale, use stratified sampling based on confidence tiers:

  • Higher sampling rate for low-confidence outputs (more human review where uncertainty is greatest)
  • Lower sampling rate for high-confidence routine extractions (efficient use of human reviewer time)
  • This ensures quality assurance effort is concentrated where it has the most impact

Confidence Calibration

Field-level confidence scores are only useful if they are calibrated -- meaning that when Claude reports "high confidence," it should be correct at a predictable rate. Calibration requires a systematic approach:

  • Labeled validation sets: Maintain a set of documents with known-correct answers. Run Claude's extraction pipeline against this set and compare confidence scores to actual accuracy. If Claude reports "high" confidence but is wrong 20% of the time on dates, the confidence is miscalibrated.
  • Stratified random sampling: Even high-confidence extractions should be audited. Use stratified sampling across confidence tiers -- higher sampling rates for low confidence, but never zero sampling for high confidence. This catches systematic miscalibration that labeled sets alone may miss.

Accuracy Segmentation

Segment accuracy metrics by multiple dimensions to identify systematic weaknesses. Analyze accuracy by document type and by field segment to uncover patterns invisible in aggregate metrics:

  • Document type: Different formats have different extraction reliability (e.g., structured forms vs. free-text reports)
  • Field type: Names vs. numbers vs. dates vs. free text -- each has distinct failure modes
  • Source quality: OCR quality, formatting consistency, scan resolution
  • Cross-segment analysis: Some fields may be accurate in one document type but unreliable in another -- aggregate metrics hide this

Code Review Harness: Finding + Filtering

Anthropic's official pattern for code review separates two distinct phases:

Report every issue you find, including ones you are uncertain about or
consider low-severity. Do not filter for importance or confidence at this
stage - a separate verification step will do that. Your goal here is
coverage: it is better to surface a finding that later gets filtered out
than to silently drop a real bug. For each finding, include your
confidence level and an estimated severity so a downstream filter can
rank them.

The key insight: separate the finding step (maximize recall) from the filtering step (maximize precision). Moving confidence filtering out of the finding step often helps overall accuracy. Claude Opus 4.7 shows 11 percentage points better recall on hard bug-finding evaluations using this pattern.

Key Takeaways
  • Escalation triggers: checkpoints, blockers, max iterations — NOT sentiment
  • Reversible actions proceed autonomously; irreversible actions require confirmation
  • Field-level confidence (High/Medium/Low) enables targeted human review
  • Stratified sampling concentrates QA effort on low-confidence outputs
  • Code review: separate finding (recall) from filtering (precision) for best results
  • Opus 4.7: 11pp improvement in bug-finding recall with the finding+filtering pattern
Decision Guide: When to Escalate
Did the customer explicitly request a human agent?
Yes →
Escalate immediately
No →
Is there a policy gap or ambiguity Claude can’t resolve?
Yes →
Escalate to human
No →
Is the trigger based on sentiment analysis or model confidence?
Yes →
Do NOT escalate (anti-pattern — unreliable signals)
No →
Continue automated handling
summary: Escalation should be triggered by explicit criteria: checkpoints, blockers, and max iterations. The reversibility heuristic distinguishes local reversible actions (proceed autonomously) from irreversible/shared actions (require confirmation). Field-level confidence (High/Medium/Low) enables stratified random sampling for efficient QA. Accuracy segmentation by document type, field type, and source quality identifies systematic weaknesses. The code review harness separates finding (maximize recall) from filtering (maximize precision), yielding 11pp improvement in Opus 4.7 bug-finding.

key_concepts.definition: Three explicit triggers: checkpoints (predetermined review points), blockers (unresolvable issues), and max iterations (safety stopping conditions).
Reversible actions (file edits, tests) proceed autonomously; irreversible actions (force push, deletion, shared systems) require human confirmation.
Assigning High/Medium/Low confidence to individual extracted fields rather than entire documents, enabling targeted human review.
QA technique that applies higher sampling rates to low-confidence outputs and lower rates to high-confidence outputs for efficient review.
Code review pattern that separates the finding step (maximize recall, report everything) from the filtering step (maximize precision, rank by severity).
Classifies inputs by type and directs to specialized handlers with task-specific prompts, tools, and escalation thresholds.

title: Error Propagation in Agent Systems
content:
Error Propagation Challenges

In autonomous agent systems, errors do not simply fail and stop — they propagate and compound. An uncorrected error in one step becomes the faulty foundation for subsequent reasoning, leading to cascading failures that are increasingly difficult to diagnose. Anthropic explicitly warns about the "potential for compounding errors" in autonomous systems and recommends careful design to contain them.

Structured Error Context

When errors occur in agent systems, propagate structured context that enables informed recovery decisions. Every error should carry:

FieldDescriptionExample
Failure typeClassification of what went wrongtimeout, permission_denied, parse_error
Attempted queryWhat the agent was trying to doSELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 42
Partial resultsAny data retrieved before failureFirst 50 rows returned before timeout
Recovery actionWhat was attempted to fix itRetried with smaller page size
 
{
  "error": {
    "failure_type": "timeout",
    "attempted_query": "SELECT * FROM users WHERE active = true",
    "partial_results": "Retrieved 50 of estimated 10000 rows",
    "recovery_action": "Will retry with LIMIT 100 and pagination"
  }
}

Access Failure vs. Valid Empty Result

One of the most critical distinctions in error handling is between two fundamentally different outcomes that can look superficially similar:

OutcomeMeaningCorrect Response
Access failureTool could not reach the data source (network error, permission denied, timeout)Retry, escalate, or try alternative path
Valid empty resultTool successfully queried but found no matching dataAccept as legitimate answer — the absence of data IS the result
Anti-Pattern: Conflating Empty with Error

Treating a valid empty result as an error leads to infinite retry loops (the query will always return empty because there is no data). Treating an access failure as an empty result leads to false conclusions based on missing data. Both anti-patterns compound quickly in multi-step agent workflows.

Local Recovery Before Escalation

From Anthropic's Building Effective Agents guide, agents should attempt recovery locally before escalating to human operators or orchestrators:

  1. Retry with modification: Adjust parameters, reduce scope, try alternative approach
  2. Use tool results as feedback: After receiving tool results, "carefully reflect on their quality and determine optimal next steps before proceeding"
  3. Self-check before completing: "Before you finish, verify your answer against [test criteria]"
  4. Escalate only when local recovery fails: Clear criteria for when to stop retrying and seek help

Poka-Yoke in Tool Design

Poka-yoke (error-proofing) is a design principle borrowed from manufacturing that makes errors structurally harder to commit. Anthropic's team applied this during SWE-bench development:

  • Absolute file paths: Requiring absolute filepaths eliminated relative path errors entirely
  • Schema validation: Using strict: true on tool definitions guarantees inputs match the expected schema
  • Self-contained tool descriptions: Tools should be unambiguous with clear boundaries from similar tools
  • Format proximity: Tool input/output formats should match natural text patterns to reduce formatting errors

The SWE-bench team reported spending "more time optimizing tools than the overall prompt," underscoring that tool interface design is the highest-leverage error prevention investment.

Compounding Errors

Autonomous agent systems are uniquely susceptible to error compounding because each step builds on previous outputs:

  • A misclassified input routes to the wrong handler, which produces a plausible-but-wrong result
  • An undetected timeout returns partial data that is treated as complete
  • A hallucinated fact gets incorporated into a progress file and persists across sessions

Mitigation strategies include:

  • Verification at each step: Use tool results as ground truth; never assume success
