  • Sandboxed testing: "Extensive testing in sandboxed environments with appropriate guardrails"
  • Structured state files: Use tests.json patterns so that facts can be verified independently
  • Git checkpoints: Enable rollback when errors are detected late

The Self-Check Pattern

After receiving tool results, carefully reflect on their quality and
determine optimal next steps before proceeding.

Before you finish, verify your answer against [test criteria].
Key Takeaways
  • Errors compound in agent systems — each step builds on previous (potentially faulty) outputs
  • Structured error context (failure type, query, partial results, recovery) enables informed recovery
  • Access failures and valid empty results require fundamentally different handling
  • Local recovery before escalation: retry with modification, then self-check, then escalate
  • Poka-yoke: design tools to make errors structurally impossible (absolute paths, strict schemas)
  • SWE-bench team spent more time on tools than prompts — tool design is highest leverage
summary: Errors in agent systems propagate and compound across steps. Structured error context includes failure type, attempted query, partial results, and recovery action. The critical distinction between access failures (retry/escalate) and valid empty results (accept) prevents both infinite retries and false conclusions. Local recovery should be attempted before escalation. Poka-yoke tool design (absolute paths, strict schemas) makes errors structurally harder. The SWE-bench team spent more time optimizing tools than prompts, demonstrating that tool interface design is the highest-leverage error prevention.

key_concepts.definition: In autonomous agent systems, an uncorrected error becomes the faulty foundation for subsequent steps, leading to cascading failures that are increasingly difficult to diagnose.
Error information carrying failure type, attempted query, partial results, and recovery action to enable informed recovery decisions.
Access failure means the tool could not reach the data source (retry needed); valid empty means the query succeeded but found nothing (accept as answer).
Error-proofing principle applied to tool design: make errors structurally impossible through constraints like absolute file paths and strict schema validation.
Agents should retry with modifications, reflect on tool result quality, and self-check before escalating to human operators.
Official prompt pattern: reflect on tool result quality before proceeding, and verify final answer against test criteria before completing.

title: Information Provenance and Hallucination Prevention
content:
Why Provenance Matters

In agent systems that synthesize information from multiple sources, tracking which claims come from which sources is essential for reliability. Without provenance tracking, hallucinated information becomes indistinguishable from grounded facts — and once a hallucination enters a persistent state file, it can propagate across sessions indefinitely.

Claim-Source Mappings with XML Document Tags

Anthropic's official prompting best practices prescribe XML document tags to maintain provenance in long-context scenarios:

<documents>
  <document index="1">
    <source>annual_report_2023.pdf</source>
    <document_content>
      Revenue increased 15% year-over-year...
    </document_content>
  </document>
  <document index="2">
    <source>competitor_analysis_q2.xlsx</source>
    <document_content>
      Market share declined 2 percentage points...
    </document_content>
  </document>
</documents>

The index attribute and <source> subtag enable Claude to reference specific documents when making claims. For long document tasks, ask Claude to quote relevant parts FIRST before carrying out its task — this helps Claude "cut through the noise" of large contexts.

Quote Extraction Pattern

Find quotes from the patient records and appointment history that are
relevant to diagnosing the patient's reported symptoms. Place these in
<quotes> tags. Then, based on these quotes, list all information that
would help the doctor diagnose. Place your diagnostic information in
<info> tags.

This pattern forces Claude to ground its reasoning in actual source text before synthesizing conclusions, creating an auditable chain from source to claim.

Conflicting Statistics Annotation

When multiple sources provide contradictory data points, the correct approach is transparency:

  • Note the conflict explicitly: Do not silently pick one value
  • Report each source's claim with attribution: "Source A reports X; Source B reports Y"
  • Provide context for the discrepancy: Different time periods, methodologies, definitions

Temporal Data and Publication Dates

Track the temporal dimension of information to prevent stale data from being treated as current:

  • Note publication dates: When data was published or collected
  • Flag potentially outdated information: Especially in fast-evolving fields
  • Consider recency when synthesizing: More recent data should generally take precedence, but note the reasoning

Provenance Metadata Fields

Each extracted fact or claim in a production pipeline should carry structured provenance metadata:

FieldDescriptionExample
source_urlURL or file path of the source documents3://bucket/reports/q2_2025.pdf
publication_dateWhen the source was published or last updated2025-06-15
confidenceField-level confidence: high, medium, or lowhigh
coverageWhether the source was fully or partially processedPARTIAL COVERAGE: pages 1-10 of 25

This structured provenance enables downstream systems to filter by confidence, flag stale data (old publication_date), and identify gaps in coverage. Without these fields, hallucinated facts become indistinguishable from grounded ones after they enter persistent storage.

Coverage Gap Reporting

Equally important as reporting what was found is reporting what was not found:

  • Identify expected information that is missing from sources
  • Flag areas where sources are incomplete
  • Distinguish between "not found" (searched and absent) and "confirmed absent" (sources explicitly state the data does not exist)
  • Use PARTIAL COVERAGE tags with timeout notes when processing was truncated

Anti-Hallucination: The investigate_before_answering Pattern

Anthropic's official anti-hallucination pattern for code-related tasks:

<investigate_before_answering>
Never speculate about code you have not opened. If the user references
a specific file, you MUST read the file before answering. Make sure to
investigate and read relevant files BEFORE answering questions about the
codebase. Never make any claims about code before investigating unless
you are certain of the correct answer - give grounded and
hallucination-free answers.
</investigate_before_answering>
The Core Rule

Never speculate about code you have not opened. This principle extends beyond code: never make factual claims about data you have not loaded into context. Use tools to verify before asserting.

Research Best Practices

Anthropic's official research pattern for complex information synthesis tasks instructs agents to maintain epistemic rigor:

Search for this information in a structured way. As you gather data,
develop several competing hypotheses. Track your confidence levels in
your progress notes to improve calibration. Regularly self-critique
your approach and plan. Update a hypothesis tree or research notes
file to persist information and provide transparency. Break down this
complex research task systematically.

The key elements of this pattern:

  • Competing hypotheses: Maintain multiple explanations simultaneously rather than anchoring on the first plausible answer
  • Confidence tracking: Explicitly note confidence levels in progress files to improve calibration over time
  • Self-critique: Regularly question your own approach and assumptions
  • Persistent transparency: Update hypothesis trees or research notes files so the reasoning chain is externalized and auditable

Provenance in Practice: Agent Workflows

Combining these patterns creates a provenance-aware agent workflow:

  1. Load sources with XML tags: Each source gets an index and source identifier
  2. Extract quotes before reasoning: Ground claims in actual source text
  3. Track confidence per claim: High (directly stated), Medium (inferred), Low (speculative/conflicting)
  4. Flag conflicts transparently: Report discrepancies with attribution to each source
  5. Report gaps: Note what was expected but not found
  6. Persist to external files: Use structured notes so provenance survives compaction
Key Takeaways
  • XML document tags with index and source attributes enable claim-source mapping
  • Quote extraction pattern: extract relevant quotes FIRST, then reason from them
  • Never silently resolve conflicting statistics — report all sources with attribution
  • <investigate_before_answering> is the official anti-hallucination prompt pattern
  • Research pattern: competing hypotheses, confidence tracking, self-critique, persistent notes
  • Coverage gap reporting: distinguish "not found" from "confirmed absent"
summary: Information provenance tracks which claims come from which sources, preventing hallucinated information from propagating. XML document tags with index and source attributes enable claim-source mappings. The quote extraction pattern forces Claude to ground reasoning in actual source text. Conflicting statistics must be reported transparently with attribution. The investigate_before_answering pattern is Anthropic's official anti-hallucination prompt. Research best practices include maintaining competing hypotheses, tracking confidence levels, and persisting reasoning chains to external files for auditability.

key_concepts.definition: Using XML document tags with index and source attributes to track which claims originate from which source documents.
Ask Claude to extract relevant quotes into tagged sections FIRST, then reason from those quotes, creating an auditable chain from source to conclusion.
Anthropic's official anti-hallucination pattern: never speculate about code or data not loaded into context. Read files before making claims.
Research pattern requiring agents to maintain multiple explanations simultaneously rather than anchoring on the first plausible answer.
Reporting what was NOT found as well as what was found, distinguishing between 'not found' (searched, absent) and 'confirmed absent' (sources explicitly deny existence).
Explicitly noting confidence levels in progress files to improve calibration: high (directly stated), medium (inferred), low (speculative/conflicting).

title: Subagent Delegation for Context Management
content:
Subagents as a Context Management Strategy

Subagents are not just a delegation mechanism — they are a context management strategy. The core principle from Anthropic's documentation: use a subagent when a side task would flood your main conversation with search results, logs, or file contents you will not reference again. The subagent does that work in its own context and returns only a summary.

Each subagent runs in its own context window with a custom system prompt, specific tool access, and independent permissions. This isolation means that verbose tool outputs, file reads, and intermediate reasoning stay entirely within the subagent's context — only the condensed result returns to the main conversation.

Context Savings Example

The official documentation provides a concrete visualization of context savings:

MetricValue
Tokens consumed in subagent context6,100 tokens (file reads, tool outputs)
Tokens returned to main context420 tokens (summary only)
Context savings~93% reduction in main context consumption

The context engineering guide notes that subagent summaries typically return 1,000-2,000 tokens to the main agent, regardless of how much data was processed internally.

The Explore Subagent (Read-Only Investigation)

The built-in Explore subagent is purpose-built for context-efficient investigation:

PropertyValue
ModelHaiku (fast, low-latency, cost-efficient)
ToolsRead-only (Write and Edit denied)
PurposeFile discovery, code search, codebase exploration
Thoroughness levelsquick, medium, very thorough
CLAUDE.mdSkips loading for smaller context footprint

Because Explore uses Haiku and read-only tools, it is both cheaper and faster than delegating investigation to the main conversation's model.

Subagent Persistent Memory

Subagents can maintain their own auto memory, enabling them to accumulate knowledge across invocations:

ScopeLocationUse When
user~/.claude/agent-memory/<name>/Learnings apply across all projects
project.claude/agent-memory/<name>/Knowledge is project-specific, shareable via VCS
local.claude/agent-memory-local/<name>/Project-specific, not for version control

When memory is enabled, the first 200 lines or 25KB of MEMORY.md is loaded at subagent startup. The recommended default scope is project.

---
name: code-reviewer
description: Reviews code for quality and best practices
memory: project
---

You are a code reviewer. When invoked:
1. Check your memory for patterns you've seen before
2. Perform the review
3. Save what you learned to your memory

The /btw Alternative

For quick questions where full subagent isolation is overkill, Claude Code provides the /btw command:

  • Sees your full current context (unlike a subagent which starts fresh)
  • Has no tool access (unlike subagents which can read/write files)
  • Answer is discarded, not added to conversation history
  • Best for: quick questions about something already in your conversation

When NOT to Use Subagents

Subagent Anti-Patterns
  • Task needs frequent back-and-forth: Iterative refinement requires shared context that subagents lack
  • Multiple phases share significant context: Planning, implementation, and testing that reference each other belong in main conversation
  • Quick, targeted changes: Overhead of spawning a subagent exceeds the benefit for simple edits
  • Latency-sensitive operations: Subagents start fresh and may need time to gather context

Additionally, Anthropic warns that Claude Opus 4.6 has "a strong predilection for subagents and may spawn them in situations where a simpler, direct approach would suffice." The official guidance: use subagents when tasks can run in parallel, require isolated context, or involve independent workstreams. For simple tasks, sequential operations, or single-file edits, work directly.

Subagent Compaction (Independent from Main)

Subagents support automatic compaction using the same logic as the main conversation:

  • Default trigger: approximately 95% capacity
  • Override via CLAUDE_AUTOCOMPACT_PCT_OVERRIDE environment variable
  • Compaction events are logged in transcript files
  • Subagent transcripts are stored separately and are unaffected by main conversation compaction
  • Transcripts persist within session and survive Claude Code restarts

Subagent Context Flow

Understanding what a subagent receives (and does not receive) is critical:

  • Receives: Its own system prompt (markdown body), basic environment details, CLAUDE.md files, same MCP servers and skills setup
  • Does NOT receive: Main session's conversation history, main session's auto memory (MEMORY.md)
  • Returns: Only the final text response plus a small metadata trailer with token counts and duration
  • Cannot: Spawn other subagents (no nesting)
Key Takeaways
  • Subagents are a context management strategy: side tasks in isolated context, summary returns
  • Context savings example: 6,100 tokens in, 420 tokens out (~93% reduction)
  • Explore subagent: Haiku model, read-only, skips CLAUDE.md for minimal footprint
  • Subagent memory: 200 lines or 25KB of MEMORY.md loaded; recommended scope is project
  • /btw for quick questions: sees full context, no tools, answer discarded
  • Subagent compaction triggers at ~95% capacity, independent from main conversation
  • Opus 4.6 over-delegates to subagents — use direct approach for simple tasks
Decision Guide: Subagent vs Inline
Will the task produce verbose output (large file reads, web searches)?
Yes →
Use Subagent (protects coordinator’s context)
No →
Is it a quick, focused lookup?
Yes →
Do it Inline (avoid subagent overhead)
No →
Does it need isolated tool access?
Yes →
Use Subagent (scoped allowedTools)
No →
Do it Inline
summary: Subagents are a context management strategy: side tasks run in isolated context windows, and only summaries return to the main conversation. The official example shows 6,100 tokens consumed internally with only 420 tokens returned (~93% savings). The Explore subagent uses Haiku for fast, read-only investigation. Subagents can maintain persistent memory (user/project/local scopes, 200 lines or 25KB loaded). The /btw command handles quick questions without subagent overhead. Subagent compaction triggers independently at ~95% capacity. Avoid subagents for iterative tasks, shared-context workflows, simple edits, and latency-sensitive operations.

key_concepts.definition: Subagents run in their own context window. Verbose tool outputs stay in the subagent; only a condensed summary (typically 1,000-2,000 tokens) returns to the main conversation.
Built-in read-only subagent using Haiku model for fast, cost-efficient codebase exploration with three thoroughness levels: quick, medium, very thorough.
Official example: 6,100 tokens consumed in subagent context, 420 tokens returned to main context  --  approximately 93% reduction in main context consumption.
Three scopes (user, project, local) for accumulating knowledge across invocations. First 200 lines or 25KB of MEMORY.md loaded at startup. Recommended default: project.
Quick question alternative to subagents: sees full current context, has no tool access, answer is discarded rather than added to history.
Independent auto-compaction at ~95% capacity. Transcripts stored separately from main conversation and survive both main compaction and Claude Code restarts.
